## Page 5

### [p005_c0001] MTA PUBLIC PARTICIPATION GUIDE 2026
### [p005_c0002] 1. Introduction
[p005_c0003] Public transportation is of paramount importance to the economic life and social fabric of the New York City metropolitan area and its adjoining regions. Changes in services, fares, and schedules immediately affect large numbers of riders.
[p005_c0004] Transit agencies funded by the U.S. Department of Transportation, Federal Transit Administration (FTA) are required to submit a public participation guide that includes strategies, procedures, and outcomes that will ensure participation of all members of the public, including minorities, people with disabilities, low-income populations and people who speak English less than very well 1 , when planning for transportation decisions.
[p005_c0005] Public participation and comments before the introduction of changes in transportation services (routes, schedules, and fares) play a vital role in the Metropolitan Transportation Authority's (MTA) relationship with its customers – ensuring that all customers and local authorities have equitable access and a voice in the process of determining service changes.
[p005_c0006] Engaging in this public dialogue also enables the MTA to provide relevant information and to organize accessible and productive forums for public hearings and meetings, fulfilling legal requirements. In the interest of serving the public, the MTA holds a variety of public meetings that provide opportunities for meaningful public participation across multiple platforms, including in-person, hybrid, 2 and virtual formats. Additionally, the MTA has created various accessible and customer-friendly channels through which the public can provide feedback on MTA proposals. This enables a broader range of stakeholders to participate in the transportation decision-making process and ensures that the engagement is accessible to all to comment on service and policy changes.
[p005_c0007] _1 Based on the requirement of FTA Circular 4702.1B_
[p005_c0008] _2 A Hybrid event is one that uses a combination of in-person and digital elements, tailored to each audience for optimal experiences. This platform aims to maximize accessibility and inclusivity, allowing people who cannot attend in person due to geographic, time, or mobility constraints to still participate and contribute. Hybrid events often feature live streaming of presentations and interactive Q&A sessions._
[p005_c0009] _1_
[p005_c0010] _Master Page # 5 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 6

### [p006_c0001] 1.1 Purpose of This Guide
[p006_c0002] The MTA generates support for its financial plan and capital program while also educating the public about new products and services through open discussions. To ensure that messages are coherent and easily understood, the MTA has established this document to create written standards and procedures that are implemented across all operating agencies.
[p006_c0003] This guide outlines the practical steps for planning and implementing public participation activities. It is designed to be comprehensive and to cover all anticipated situations. MTA officials and staff involved in the planning and implementation of in-person, hybrid, and/or virtual public hearings and/or meetings are advised to familiarize themselves with the steps and procedures in this guide to ensure the engagements are inclusive of current and potential customers of the MTA service areas.
[p006_c0004] There are six (6) major purposes of this public participation guide:
- [p006_c0005] 1. To define the MTA's guidelines for public participation for in-person, hybrid and virtual meetings and/or hearings. 2. To describe the steps in planning and implementing various types of public meetings and/or hearings for in-person, hybrid and virtual. 3. To explain the legal requirements for public participation. 4. To detail the opportunities for speaking at public hearings and at MTA Board and/or committee meetings in-person and virtually. 5. To list other ways members of the public can communicate with the MTA. 6. To describe the ways MTA Board and committee actions and decisions are reported and made public.
[p006_c0006] _2_
[p006_c0007] _Master Page # 6 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 7

### [p007_c0001] 1.2 About the Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA)
[p007_c0002] The MTA is North America's largest public transportation network, serving a population of 15.3 million people across a 5,000-square-mile travel area throughout New York City (all five boroughs), Long Island, southeastern New York State, and New Haven and Fairfield counties in the state of Connecticut.
[p007_c0003] The MTA consists of the following affiliated and subsidiary agencies: Long Island Rail Road Company (LIRR) , Metro-North Commuter Railroad Company (MNR) , MTA Bus Company (MTA BUS) , MTA Construction and Development Company (C&D) , Grand Central Madison Concourse Operating Company (GCMC) , New York City Transit Authority (NYCT) - including the Manhattan and Bronx Surface Transit Operating Authority (MaBSTOA) and the Staten Island Rapid Transit Operating Authority (SIRTOA) - and the MTA Triborough Bridge and Tunnel Authority (B&T) .
[p007_c0004] The MTA is governed by a 23-member Board. Voting members are nominated by the Governor, with four members recommended by New York City's Mayor and one each by the County Executives of Nassau, Suffolk, Westchester, Dutchess, Orange, Rockland, and Putnam counties (the members representing the latter four cast one collective vote). The Board also has six rotating non-voting seats held by representatives of organized labor and the Permanent Citizens Advisory Committee (PCAC), which serves as a voice for users of MTA transit and the commuter railroads.
[p007_c0005] The MTA is led by the Chair and CEO supported by a strong leadership team. Acting on behalf of its constituent agencies, the MTA Board sets policy, and Headquarters secures operating and capital funding, coordinates the capital program, oversees planning, and administers the MTA's real estate operations.
[p007_c0006] The MTA network includes the nation's largest bus fleet and more subway and commuter rail cars than all other U.S. transit systems combined. Metro-North Railroad also operates under contracts in Connecticut's Fairfield and New Haven counties and on the New York portion of the Port Jervis and Pascack Valley lines of New Jersey Transit.
[p007_c0007] Population Served: MTA buses and trains move 15.3 million customers a year - nearly one in every four transit users in the United States. Its bridges and tunnels move 336 million vehicles annually, making the MTA the nation's largest toll authority.
[p007_c0008] _3_
[p007_c0009] _Master Page # 7 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 8

[p008_c0001] Enhancing the quality of life and economic health of the region, the MTA provides cost-efficient, safe, reliable, and accessible transportation services. Each year, MTA connects the region's residents and visitors to health care, work, schools, sporting events, concerts, beaches, Broadway shows, and a multitude of other attractions around the New York metropolitan region.
### [p008_c0002] 1.3 The Importance of Public Participation
[p008_c0003] The MTA defines its public participation audience as anyone who resides, works, visits, has an interest in, or does business in an area potentially affected by transportation decisions. In addition to individuals, this audience also includes community-based organizations, neighborhood and civic associations, local government and advocacy groups.
[p008_c0004] Community Voices and Feedback: Public participation ensures input from a wide range of community members, leading to fairer outcomes and transportation solutions that address varied needs.
[p008_c0005] Enhanced Project Outcomes: Input from the public can help identify community needs, priorities, and potential issues decision-makers may otherwise overlook. This can lead to better-designed projects that are more efficient and effective.
[p008_c0006] Transparency: Engaging the public promotes transparency in the decision-making process and strengthens trust between government agencies or transportation authorities and the community, as people see their concerns being acknowledged and addressed.
[p008_c0007] Reduced Conflicts: Early and meaningful engagement with the public can help identify and resolve potential conflicts before they escalate, reducing the risk of costly delays or opposition during project implementation.
[p008_c0008] Community Ownership: When people are actively involved in the decision-making process, they develop a sense of ownership and are more likely to support and stand behind the final outcomes.
[p008_c0009] Legal and Policy Requirements: Many transportation projects, particularly those that use federal funds, are required by law to involve public input. Observing these requirements ensures compliance with regulations, such as the National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) and 49 U.S.C. Sections 5307(b) and (c).
[p008_c0010] _4_
[p008_c0011] _Master Page # 8 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 9

### [p009_c0001] 2. MTA Public Participation Principles
### [p009_c0002] 2.1 Equal Opportunity and Access
[p009_c0003] The MTA is committed to ensuring that our transportation services are fair for all New Yorkers. This commitment serves as the foundation for public engagement and the public participation process provides a platform for all individuals to participate, contribute and be considered during the decision-making process. To ensure that MTA's transportation services reflect community needs, the MTA collects and evaluates community demographics to determine potential impacts during the decision-making process.
[p009_c0004] The MTA's approach to equal opportunity and access in the public participation process includes:
[p009_c0005] Inclusive Engagement: Creating an open and welcoming environment where all individuals, including populations that speak English less than very well 3 , people with disabilities, minorities and low-income communities , are encouraged to participate in MTA decision-making processes. The process includes open forums such as community engagement, public hearings, committee and board meetings.
[p009_c0006] Language Access: The MTA service area has customers who speak multiple languages, many of whom speak English less than very well. To ensure that community members can fully engage in all MTA decision making processes, materials and resources are translated into multiple languages. Interpreters and captioners are also made available to ensure no one is excluded based on language needs.
[p009_c0007] ADA Compliance: MTA ensures compliance with standards set by the American with Disabilities Act (ADA) when conducting public hearings and/or meetings. This includes ensuring that people with disabilities have equal access to meeting locations, materials in Braille (upon request), and opportunities to provide input.
[p009_c0008] Community-Centered Approach: The MTA engages the public through community boards and local communities to meet residents where they are and to make participation as easy and effective as possible. Through active outreach, the MTA connects with all communities via in-person, virtual, hybrid meetings and public hearings. The MTA also maintains ongoing partnerships with local officials, to ensure broad, inclusive and meaningful participation.
[p009_c0009] _3 This term is defined in the Federal Transit Administration's Title VI Circular (FTA Circular 4702.1B), Section 5, which addresses Limited English Proficient (LEP) populations. The term is used throughout this public participation guide._
[p009_c0010] _5_
[p009_c0011] _Master Page # 9 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 10

[p010_c0001] Data Assessment: The MTA Department of Diversity and Civil Rights regularly evaluates the MTA's public participation efforts and agency decisions to ensure equitable outreach, confirms that all communities have been fairly engaged and accessed for potential impacts of decisions on those communities.
[p010_c0002] The MTA's public participation process is not just a compliance obligation; it is a core value that seeks to amplify the needs and perspectives of every community the agency services. The MTA is dedicated to creating a transportation system that serves all residents, with the voices of the public guiding and shaping transportation decisions.
### [p010_c0003] 2.2 Accessibility and Accommodation
[p010_c0004] The COVID 19 pandemic presented an opportunity for the MTA to rethink the way public hearings and meetings were conducted to ensure that all individuals can be comfortable participating in the public engagement process. As part of this commitment, the agency continues to promote accessibility and provide accommodation, efforts which include:
[p010_c0005] American with Disabilities Act (ADA) Compliance: All public meetings and hearings are held in ADA-accessible venues, ensuring full usability by people with disabilities, including wheelchair and scooter users.
[p010_c0006] Virtual Participation Options: In addition to in-person meetings, the MTA offers virtual options that allow individuals to participate remotely. These options are equipped with accessibility features, such as closed captioning and compatibility with screen readers, to ensure full access for people with hearing or visual disabilities.
[p010_c0007] Assistive Listening Devices (ALDs): For in-person events held at MTA Headquarters, assistive listening devices are made available to People with Hearing Loss or who are Hard of Hearing to enhance audio clarity during public hearings, committee and/or board meetings.
[p010_c0008] Sign Language Interpretation: American Sign Language (ASL) interpreters are available for all public hearings and meetings. This ensures that People who are Deaf or Hard of Hearing can fully engage in the proceedings. If specific accommodation is needed, the MTA encourages participants to submit a request for this accommodation at least five (5) business days in advance of the scheduled meeting or hearing date using one of the following available communication methods:
[p010_c0009] _6_
[p010_c0010] _Master Page # 10 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 11

- [p011_c0001] • 711-relay service for a communication assistant to facilitate a call to the hotline. • Public Hearing Hotline on 646-252-6777. • Zoom Webinar telephone number. • Via postal mail to: MTA Government & Community Relations, 2 Broadway, D16.94, New York, NY 10004. • Via email through the mta.info portal.
[p011_c0002] Translations Services: MTA provides translations and interpretation services at community meetings and public hearings to ensure meaningful access for participants. Written materials are generally available in multiple languages. Oral interpretation must be requested in advance using the contact methods listed above.
### [p011_c0003] Interpretation
[p011_c0004] Language assistance services utilize various modes of interpretation to ensure effective communication across different languages and cultural contexts.
[p011_c0005] These modes include:
### [p011_c0006] 1. Consecutive Interpretation
- [p011_c0007] • The interpreter listens to a speaker's message in one language, takes notes if needed, and then conveys the message in the target language after the speaker pauses. • Commonly used in one-on-one meetings, interviews, or legal settings.
### [p011_c0008] 2. Simultaneous Interpretation
- [p011_c0009] • The interpreter translates the message in real-time while the speaker continues to talk. • Generally considered the most popular in formal contexts due to speed and efficiency. • Often used in conferences, large meetings, or events where speed is critical. • Requires specialized equipment, such as headsets and microphones, for effective communication.
### [p011_c0010] 3. Over-the-Phone Interpretation (OPI)
- [p011_c0011] • Interpretation conducted over the telephone providing immediate assistance for remote communication. • Frequently used in customer service, emergency services, and medical settings.
### [p011_c0012] 4. Relay Interpretation
- [p011_c0013] • Used when there are multiple languages involved, requiring interpretation from the source language to an intermediary language, and then into the target language. • Common in international conferences.
[p011_c0014] _7_
[p011_c0015] _Master Page # 11 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 12

### [p012_c0001] 5. Video Remote Interpretation (VRI)
- [p012_c0002] • Like OPI interpretation but uses video technology to allow interpreters to see the participants and any non-verbal cues.
### [p012_c0003] 6. On-Site (In-Person Interpretation)
- [p012_c0004] • The interpreter is physically present at the location of the public hearing or meeting.
### [p012_c0005] 7. American Sign Language (ASL) Interpretation
- [p012_c0006] • For People who are Deaf or Hard of Hearing, interpreters translate spoken language into sign language. • Can be performed simultaneously or consecutively.
### [p012_c0007] Engaging with a Vendor for Interpretation Services
[p012_c0008] When engaging with a vendor to procure interpretation services, the following steps are required:
- [p012_c0009] • Confirm Language Needs: Identify the language(s) required. • Share Content: Provide the details, including any written materials, about the meeting. Details include, but are not limited to, the topic to be discussed, the expected duration of the meeting, and any industry-specific terminology/acronyms (i.e., FHWA – Federal Highway Administration, CBDTP – Central Business District Tolling Program, etc.). • Book in Advance: Real-time interpretation requires advance booking (i.e. 3 – 5 business days) to ensure availability. Be sure to allow time for technical set-up and assign someone to meet the interpreter and answer any questions. • Confirm In-Person or Virtual: Schedule the interpreter for on-site or remote interpretation services. Remote interpretation usually provides more flexibility when requesting services. • Requirements: Confirm that the vendor and interpreters can accommodate specific needs of the participants.
[p012_c0010] Alternative Formats for Materials: All informational materials, including meeting agendas, presentations (using “alt text 4 ”), and reports, are available in alternative formats such as large print, Braille, and digital versions compatible with screen readers. Requests regarding these formats should be made in advance to ensure that materials are accessible to all participants.
[p012_c0011] _4 Alt Text – a written text description of an image embedded into the image itself, not visible on screen – that screen readers identify and read aloud to users. This lets someone who cannot see the photo understand what it adds to the document. (Accessible Content and the MTA)_
[p012_c0012] _8_
[p012_c0013] _Master Page # 12 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 13

[p013_c0001] Remote Comment Submission: For individuals unable to attend in-person or virtually, the MTA offers alternative methods for submitting comments, including by telephone, email, postal mail or the agency's on-line portal. This ensures that everyone can share their input regardless of physical location or ability to attend in-person.
[p013_c0002] Advance Notice of Accommodations: To ensure that accommodations are provided effectively, notices to the public include language encouraging participants to contact us at least five business days in advance to request an accommodation.
[p013_c0003] The MTA is dedicated to removing barriers and fostering a process that reflects the needs of its service areas. By making accessibility and accommodations a core component of its public participation efforts, the MTA creates an environment where every individual can engage meaningfully in the decisions that shape the region's transportation system.
> [p013_c0004] A large, solid blue arrow pointing to the right, positioned horizontally across the page. It has a slight 3D effect with a darker blue shadow on the right side.
### [p013_c0005] 3. Public Communication and Outreach
### [p013_c0006] 3.1 Communication Tools (Digital Platforms / Social Media)
[p013_c0007] The MTA prioritizes open, clear, and accessible communication with the public utilizing various channels to provide timely and accurate information and obtain maximum participation in our decision-making processes. The following guidelines outline the MTA's approach to public communication, including the use of digital tools, public notices, language access, and distribution practices.
### [p013_c0008] Digital Platforms and Social Media
[p013_c0009] MTA digital platforms and social media channels serve as key tools to keep the public informed, offer real-time updates, and facilitate dialogue with the community.
[p013_c0010] The MTA shares information through its website, mta.info, official MTA App, and through various social media platforms. These platforms provide easy access to meeting schedules, project updates, service changes, and opportunities for public input.
[p013_c0011] _9_
[p013_c0012] _Master Page # 13 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 14

### [p014_c0001] 3.2 Public Notices and Announcements
[p014_c0002] Notification Methods: The MTA issues public notices and announcements for hearings, meetings, and major service updates through multiple outlets. Public notices and announcements are essential to keeping local communities informed about important developments, changes in services, and upcoming opportunities for public involvement. The MTA posts notices on its website, shares with community boards, and distributes them through multiple outlets to reach a broad audience. Areas of distribution include, but are not limited to, digital and print media, systemwide postings in common areas, and digital ads for subway and buses, stations and train cars. Additionally, physical notices are posted at transit stations and on paratransit vehicles for increased visibility.
[p014_c0003] The MTA issues press releases for major projects, fair and toll proposals, and policy changes. Announcements are accompanied by informational materials that provide background, explain the reasoning behind any proposed changes, and outline how the public can participate in related hearing or comment periods. This multi-channel approach allows the MTA to reach a wide audience, ensuring critical information is accessible to the entire community. Public notices are typically posted well in advance of significant events, giving individuals enough time to review details, request additional information/translated material/prepare feedback, and attend meetings.
[p014_c0004] Timing: To allow ample time for public planning and participation, legal public notices are typically published at least 30 days in advance of scheduled public hearing(s) (excluding eminent domain public hearings which are at least 15 days in advance of scheduled public hearing).
### [p014_c0005] 3.3 Distribution of Clear and Concise Information
[p014_c0006] In 2010, the US Federal Government enacted the Plain Language Act. It requires federally funded agencies to “use clear communication that the public can understand and use.”
[p014_c0007] All MTA public communications are designed to be clear, concise and accessible to readers of varied backgrounds and educational levels. Avoiding technical jargon, the MTA aims to present information in straightforward language that highlights essential details. The MTA writes content at an eighth grade reading level.
[p014_c0008] Content Development: Making MTA content accessible supports the agency’s strategic priorities which include delivering better service, promoting safety and respect, effective customer communication, and increasing appeal for customers.
[p014_c0009] _10_
[p014_c0010] _Master Page # 14 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 15

[p015_c0001] Accessible Content: Accessible design follows principles such as using plain language that can be easily understood on the first read. Notices are crafted to include essential information – such as the date, time and location, and purpose of the event – presented in a straightforward and accessible format. Additional information on accessibility, accommodation, and language assistance is also provided. Notices are written using inclusive, gender-neutral, people-first language in an accessible format.
[p015_c0002] Multiple Formats: Information is provided in multiple formats – digital, print, and audio – to accommodate a variety of preferences and needs. For People who are Blind or with Low Vision, the MTA offers digital formats compatible with screen readers, large-print materials and Public Hearing notice in Braille also available with advance notice by contacting the public hearing hotline.
[p015_c0003] Direct Communication Channels: We provide direct contact information on all public announcements, allowing community members to reach MTA representative for clarification or further information.
[p015_c0004] Through these communication guidelines, the MTA is committed to enhancing accessibility and transparency across all public engagement efforts. The agency's approach ensures that every customer can stay informed, connected with MTA initiatives, and participate meaningfully in shaping the future of the transportation system.
### [p015_c0005] 4. Categories of Public Participation
[p015_c0006] The MTA uses a categorization system, in which categories 1-2 outline the process for conducting public hearings required by MTA Board, legal, or regulatory requirements. Categories 3-5 govern how the MTA schedules other public meetings or hearings that may not be legally required but are essential for strengthening dialogue between the MTA and the public.
### [p015_c0007] 4.1 Public Hearings
[p015_c0008] Public hearings are meetings that allow members of the community to be formally heard and have their comments recorded.
[p015_c0009] The first level of public participation, includes in-person, hybrid, or virtual public hearings and/or meetings required by law before implementing proposed major changes in service, which are considered major when meeting at least one of the following criteria aggregated over a 12 month period: 25% or greater change
[p015_c0010] _11_
[p015_c0011] _Master Page # 15 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 16

[p016_c0001] in daily revenue miles involving a reduction in operating hours that exceed one hour a day or, the partial or complete closing of a subway or commuter railroad station.
[p016_c0002] By state and/or federal law, the MTA is obligated to hold public hearings on the following:
- [p016_c0003] • Major bus, subway, and commuter rail service changes. • Fare and Toll Changes. • Subway and commuter rail station access changes/closings. • Eminent Domain proceedings (property acquisitions). • Federal Transit Administration funding requests. • Environmental Impact Statements (EIS). • Capital Programs.
### [p016_c0004] Required Public Hearings or Public Involvement
[p016_c0005] Consistent with the Circular, early and continuous opportunities for public review and comment should be provided at key decision points throughout the process to ensure meaningful public participation to ensure meaningful public participation.
[p016_c0006] Public Hearings or public involvement are required under the following authorities:
### [p016_c0007] MTA Board, Federal Law, and Title VI
[p016_c0008] MTA Board approved Service Standards and Policies, in accordance with Title VI of the Civil Rights Acts of 1964 and FTA Title VI Circular 4702 1B, the MTA shall provide a meaningful opportunity for public comment for major service changes.
### [p016_c0009] Major Bus or Subway Service Changes are defined as those resulting in:
- [p016_c0010] ▪ Route restructuring actions resulting in at least a 25% change in overall route length. ▪ Service frequency changes resulting in at least a 25% change in annual revenue vehicle miles (RVM). Annual RVM is a compilation of Weekday, Saturday, and Sunday RVMs; or ▪ Span change actions resulting in at least one hour change in service span.
[p016_c0011] _12_
[p016_c0012] _Master Page # 16 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 17

### [p017_c0001] Commuter Rail Major Service Changes are defined as those resulting in:
- [p017_c0002] • The addition or deletion of trains is estimated to carry 25% or more of the total line ridership on the day of the week for which the change is made. • The addition or deletion of trains or cars constituting more than 25% of the revenue car miles operated on the line on the day of the week for which the change is proposed. • The establishment or discontinuance of service over a line segment. • The establishment or discontinuance of a station; or • A change in frequency of service to a station on a given day by more than 25%.
### [p017_c0003] Operating and/or Capital Assistance Projects
[p017_c0004] The MTA is required by federal transit laws outlined in Title 49, Chapter 53, Section 5307(b) and Section 5307(c)(1)(1) of the United States Code, and 23 CFR 450.326(b), to provide an opportunity for a public hearing or conduct a public hearing when applying for federal operating and/or capital assistance projects.
### [p017_c0005] Fare Changes (Including Fare Media Changes)
[p017_c0006] In accordance with U.S.C. Title 49, Chapter 53, Section 5307(d)(1), recipients of Section 5307 and 5337 grants are obligated to establish a “locally developed process” for actively seeking and considering public input prior to implementing fare changes. The MTA has traditionally relied on public hearings as the basis of its “locally developed process” to gather public feedback on fare change proposals.
[p017_c0007] Apart from utilizing the “locally developed process” guidelines for gathering public input on fare change proposals, the MTA’s Government & Community Relations (GCR) department has extended the application of these standards to include the planning and execution of public hearings for significant changes in service, fares, and tolls. This process was officially established by the MTA Board.
### [p017_c0008] Environmental Justice (EJ)
[p017_c0009] USDOT funding recipients must meet the requirements of meaningful public involvement and participation under Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, and the National Environmental Policy Act of 1969 (NEPA), and other existing requirements. The MTA seeks to increase engagement in EJ Areas and to ensure that community participation reflects the MTA’s local service area population. The MTA is committed to involving these communities in a meaningful way throughout the planning process, particularly when regulated activities are proposed in their neighborhoods or when existing facilities expand their operations.
[p017_c0010] _13_
[p017_c0011] _Master Page # 17 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 18

### [p018_c0001] Eminent Domain Hearings
[p018_c0002] The requirements for a public hearing on proposals in which the MTA or an operating agency invokes the power of eminent domain (condemnation) are set forth in the New York State Eminent Domain Procedure Law (EDPL), Article 2. The requirements are procedures for condemnation described in Sections 201-204.
### [p018_c0003] Request for Federal Financial Assistance Hearings
[p018_c0004] As a recipient of federal financial assistance from the Federal Transit Administration, the MTA is required to hold a public hearing pursuant to 49 USC Section 5307(b).
### [p018_c0005] Hearings to Establish New Services
[p018_c0006] As provided in Section 1266(2) of the New York Public Authorities Law, the MTA or any of its subsidiaries may establish a new transportation service within the metropolitan commuter transportation district, which is defined as the 14-county region served by the MTA. Out of the 14 counties, two are in Connecticut and the Connecticut Department of Transportation (CDOT) is responsible for public hearings in those counties.
### [p018_c0007] 4.2 Public Speaking at MTA Board and Committee Meetings
[p018_c0008] Includes descriptions of opportunities and related procedures for public speaking available to members of the public at MTA board and committee meetings, as required under the New York Public Authorities Law.
### [p018_c0009] Registration Process for Public Speaking
[p018_c0010] The MTA board and its committees hold in-person meetings over two days, typically the fourth week of each month, except for August, when board or committee meetings are not usually held. Exact dates and times are posted on the board and committee meeting webpage. Please note that schedules are subject to change.
[p018_c0011] Board and committee meetings are held in person at the MTA Board Room, 2 Broadway, 20th Floor. They are also livestreamed on YouTube and recordings are posted on the website.
### [p018_c0012] Timing and Structure of Public Comments
[p018_c0013] Public comments are time limited to ensure all speakers have an opportunity to address the board or committee members. Speakers are encouraged to keep remarks focused and relevant to the meeting's agenda. This structure not only facilitates an orderly discussion but allows board members to hear a wide range of views within the meetings limited timeframe.
[p018_c0014] _14_
[p018_c0015] _Master Page # 18 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 19

### [p019_c0001] Board Meetings
[p019_c0002] Individuals who wish to speak at the board meeting must register, either online or in person at MTA Headquarters, 2 Broadway, New York City. The registration window opens 35 minutes before the scheduled start of a Board meeting and closes 5 minutes before scheduled start time.
[p019_c0003] Remote speakers may provide comments live, during the meeting, but must register to do so. Public speakers who have registered to provide live remote comments will receive an e-mail with instructions on how to access the meeting's remote public comment virtual session.
[p019_c0004] The Board meeting includes up to 60 minutes of in-person and remote comments, combined. Registered speakers beyond that time frame are invited to separately record their comments. All comments are delivered to the MTA Board and posted online.
[p019_c0005] All speakers must adhere to a two-minute limit and speaking time may not be transferred.
[p019_c0006] *Up to an additional two minutes will be provided for public speakers who need interpretation assistance or have a speech-related disability.
### [p019_c0007] Committee Meetings
[p019_c0008] Those who wish to speak at the committee meetings must register ahead of time. Registration opens 20 minutes before and closes 5 minutes before the scheduled start of each committee meeting.
[p019_c0009] Public speakers can make their comments in person at any committee meeting. To accommodate those who cannot travel to 2 Broadway, all committee meetings accommodate both in person and remote live public comments. Once individuals have registered to provide live remote comments, they will receive an email with instructions on how to access the meeting's remote public comment virtual session.
[p019_c0010] Committee meetings include up to 30 minutes of in-person and remote comments combined. Registered speakers beyond that time frame are invited to separately record their comments. All comments are delivered to the committee and posted online.
[p019_c0011] _15_
[p019_c0012] _Master Page # 19 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 20

[p020_c0001] There is a two-minute speaking limit and speaking time may not be transferred.
[p020_c0002] *Up to an additional two minutes will be provided for public speakers who need interpretation assistance or have a speech-related disability.
### [p020_c0003] Conduct During Board and Committee Meetings
[p020_c0004] Respectful and professional conduct is essential for productive interactions at board and committee meetings. The MTA provides guidelines for public conduct and decorum to maintain a positive atmosphere, ensuring that all voices can be heard in a respectful setting.
[p020_c0005] The following Rules of Conduct and Decorum apply to members of the public who speak at or attend MTA Board and Committee meetings virtually or in-person. If a member of the public violates these rules, the MTA may prevent the violator from continuing to speak, remove them from the premises, prohibit their future attendance, or restrict the camera or microphone of a virtual attendee.
- [p020_c0006] 1. Each person who wishes to speak shall register in advance, state their name and any organization they represent and identify the topics or action items in the meeting work plan or materials on which they will speak. 2. Each speaker shall adhere to the time allotted to speakers at that meeting. Time may not be transferred from one speaker to another. 3. Remarks must be related to action items or subjects identified for substantive discussion in the meeting work plan or materials for that meeting. 4. Speakers shall address their remarks only to the chairperson of the meeting and shall not make personal, impertinent, insulting, or slanderous statements about any member of the MTA Board or staff, or about other members of the public. 5. Each person who wishes to attend an MTA Board or Committee meeting in person must register in the lobby of the building and pass through security to be permitted entry. 6. Members of the public who attend a meeting in person may bring in water or other non-alcoholic beverages for personal consumption. 7. Members of the public must stay within the area designated for the public and may not enter any area reserved for MTA Board Members and staff. 8. Members of the public may not use profane or vulgar language, make any threats, or engage in other behavior that could disrupt the orderly conduct of the meeting or threaten safety.
[p020_c0007] _16_
[p020_c0008] _Master Page # 20 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 21

- [p021_c0001] 9. Members of the public may not display materials promoting or opposing any political party or any candidate for or incumbent of federal, state, judicial, or local government offices while speaking. 10. Members of the public, including public speakers, may not display or distribute banners, signs, or flyers within the meeting room.
### [p021_c0002] Emergency Evacuation Procedure for People with Disabilities
[p021_c0003] Before the start of the first committee and board meetings, an emergency evacuation announcement is made to ensure that all attendees, including those with disabilities, are informed of the evacuation procedures from the meeting space. A sample announcement is provided below:
### [p021_c0004] Emergency Evacuation Procedure Announcement
[p021_c0005] “Your safety is of the foremost importance to the MTA. Therefore, we ask that you listen and adhere to the following instructions:
[p021_c0006] If you witness an emergency, notify emergency personnel in the room and call “9-1-1” immediately. Please follow any audible instructions provided through the public address system or visually on screens in the event of an emergency. If an alarm sounds, wait for a public address announcement, and follow instructions.
[p021_c0007] If told to go to another floor, or to evacuate the building, leave all unessential items behind, and use stairwell A just across the main hallway, or Stairwell B down the hallway, past the elevators. If you have a mobility disability or cannot self-evacuate, please proceed to stairwell D or E for assistance by MTA staff or emergency personnel.
[p021_c0008] An automated external defibrillator (AED) for use by trained personnel is in the main hallway just past the elevators.
[p021_c0009] If you need assistance during an evacuation, please tell an MTA staff member or emergency personnel.
[p021_c0010] Thank you and have a safe day.”
### [p021_c0011] 4.3 Freedom of Information Law (FOIL)
[p021_c0012] The MTA encourages members of the public to obtain records maintained by the MTA, under New York’s Freedom of Information Law (FOIL, Public Officer’s Law, Article 6, Sections 84-90). Through FOIL, MTA records concerning its operations, final decisions and policies are available for public access including audio and visual recordings, official transcripts, comments, and much more. This access is subject to certain limited exceptions, including commercially sensitive material, material whose release could jeopardize safety and security, certain
[p021_c0013] _17_
[p021_c0014] _Master Page # 21 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 22

[p022_c0001] non-factual communications and others. The MTA has established a consolidated FOIL office to ensure consistent and uniform compliance with this law.
### [p022_c0002] How to Submit FOIL Requests
[p022_c0003] The MTA actively promotes transparency and accountability through its FOIL Office.
### [p022_c0004] Online
[p022_c0005] The best way to submit a FOIL request is through the MTA's online portal mta.info/transparency/foil . FOIL requests for the MTA and related agencies are handled by a centralized team.
### [p022_c0006] U.S. Postal Mail
[p022_c0007] Members of the public may also submit a request via U.S. mail to the following address: FOIL Team, MTA Legal Department, 2 Broadway, 4th Floor, New York, NY 10004.
### [p022_c0008] 4.4 Community Information Meetings and Forums
[p022_c0009] Community Information Meeting and Forums addresses proposals that would result in minor changes in service, which meet all three criteria and are defined as those resulting in less than 25% change in daily revenue miles, less than one hour service span reduction or any same-day service span increase and, less than 25% change in route miles, aggregated over a 12-month period.
### [p022_c0010] 4.5 Other Channels of Communication
[p022_c0011] This category provides a general description of additional customer information procedures including use of the Internet, digital display advertising, social media platforms and other opportunities to communicate with the MTA.
[p022_c0012] Advertisements must be placed in daily English-language publications and in multilingual weekly newspapers representing the nine most common non-English languages in the MTA's service area. Notices must also be posted on digital screens throughout the subway system, including on local and express buses to inform customers of any changes or public hearings.
[p022_c0013] _18_
[p022_c0014] _Master Page # 22 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 23

[p023_c0001] The level and extent of public participation for service, fare, toll, and other changes are guided by legal requirements or by established MTA internal practices. In all cases, the MTA seeks to meet or exceed the requirements of the law regarding public participation and has committed itself to providing accessible sites for in-person, hybrid and/or virtual public hearings and/or meetings.
[p023_c0002] To the extent possible, all advertising of the legal notice should be placed at least 30 days before the date of the hearing(s). Publications selected for Notice of the Public Hearing advertisements are:
[p023_c0003] NY Daily News NY Post The Staten Island Advance Newsday (for LIRR Proposals) amMetro New York Journal News (for MNR Proposals) El Diario (Spanish) CT Media Group (for MNR proposals impacting CT)*
[p023_c0004] *CT Media Group papers include CT Post, Stamford Advocate, Greenwich Times, Danbury News Times, and Norwalk Hour.
[p023_c0005] Advertisement in non-English publications are selected for Notice of Public Hearing. These publications include:
[p023_c0006] El Diario (Spanish Language) El Especial (Spanish Language) Haiti Libre (Creole Language) Chinese World Journal (Chinese Language) Korean Daily NY (Korean Language) Russkaya Reklama (Russian Language) Weekly Bangalee (Bangla Language) Nowy Dzien (Polish Language) Allewaa Alarabi (Arabic Language) Der Yid Weekly (Yiddish Language) Luso Americano (Portuguese Language)
[p023_c0007] Currently, the MTA retains Miller Advertising, a professional advertising firm, to design and publish its "Notice of Public Hearing" advertisements.

## Page 24

### [p024_c0001] 5. Public Hearings and Meetings
### [p024_c0002] 5.1 Public Hearing Format
[p024_c0003] The MTA offers multiple formats for public hearings to ensure broad participation. Public hearings may be held in person, virtually, or in a format that combines both in-person and virtual options. By adopting a thoughtful and accessible approach, every participant has the opportunity to engage fully and benefit from a meaningful public involvement process that supports participation.
### [p024_c0004] In-Person
[p024_c0005] In-Person hearings and meetings provide a setting where attendees can engage directly with MTA representatives, often allowing for greater interaction and community presence.
### [p024_c0006] Virtual
[p024_c0007] Virtual hearings and meetings held online are ideal for individuals who cannot attend in person due to scheduling conflicts, distance, or accessibility needs. The MTA ensures that digital platforms are accessible, providing live-streaming options, real-time captions, and language interpretation services (if requested 10 days in advance).
### [p024_c0008] Hybrid
[p024_c0009] The hybrid model maximizes flexibility, enabling participants to choose whether to attend In-person or virtually.
### [p024_c0010] 5.2 How to Find Information on Upcoming Public Hearings
[p024_c0011] To find public hearing information on the MTA website at mta.info , locate the Menu icon in the top left corner. Click on the Menu to see a drop-down list and select About , and then select Transparency . Look for a section titled Public Meetings and Hearings . Click on that tile to see a list of upcoming public hearings and details like the date, time, location, and how to join, comment or request other accommodations like the notice in Braille or language assistance services. Users can also find this information by typing “Public Meetings and Hearings” into the search bar at the top right of the homepage.
[p024_c0012] Additional information is also available by calling the MTA public hearing hotline on 646-252-6777. Telephone agents are available from 6 am – 10 pm daily. People who are Deaf or Hard of Hearing can use the Telecommunications Relay Service (TRS) by dialing 711 for a communication assistant
[p024_c0013] _20_
[p024_c0014] _Master Page # 24 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 25

[p025_c0001] to facilitate the call to the hotline. This provides an alternative way to stay informed about upcoming hearings and how to participate.
### [p025_c0002] 5.3 Legal Requirements for Public Hearings
[p025_c0003] The MTA conducts public hearings in accordance with federal and state requirements that support public participation including fare changes, major service changes, and capital projects. These requirements include specific procedures, such as providing adequate notice, using ADA accessible venues, providing opportunities for public comments (oral and written) and ensuring language services for people who speak English less than very well. Compliance with legal requirements includes meeting specific notification periods, typically advertising 30 days advance of the hearing in English language publications and in the top 9 non-English language weekly print media. (Excluding Eminent Domain public hearings).
[p025_c0004] The Agency-wide top nine (9) non-English languages include: Arabic, Bengali, Chinese, Haitian Creole, Italian, Korean, Russian, Spanish, and Yiddish.
[p025_c0005] The affidavit of publication for legal notice serves as proof of compliance with the requirement to provide adequate public notice for a public hearing. Posting on the MTA website and issuing a press release in advance does not satisfy the same notification requirements and therefore does not constitute compliance.
### [p025_c0006] Federal Transit Administration (FTA) Requirements
[p025_c0007] As a recipient of the federal funding, the MTA must comply with FTA guidelines on public involvement. Under Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the FTA's Title VI Circular, the MTA is required to conduct public outreach, especially for projects that affect communities, to prevent discrimination and to ensure access to its services.
### [p025_c0008] Environmental Regulations
[p025_c0009] The National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) and the State Environmental Quality Review Act (SEQRA) may also require public hearings for projects with environmental impacts.
[p025_c0010] _21_
[p025_c0011] _Master Page # 25 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 26

### [p026_c0001] New York State Public Authorities Law
### [p026_c0002] Section 1266 (3)
[p026_c0003] Requires the MTA to hold public hearings prior to any fare or toll increases, as well as certain service reductions. This section requires the MTA to notify the public and offers a platform for input, ensuring transparency in decisions that impact commuters.
### [p026_c0004] American with Disabilities Act (ADA) Requirements
[p026_c0005] Public hearings and meetings must comply with ADA accessibility standards to ensure people with disabilities can participate fully. This includes accessible venues, materials, and, if needed, accommodation like sign language interpreters and CART captioning must be made 10 days in advance of public meeting/hearing.
### [p026_c0006] Scheduling and Notifying the Public
[p026_c0007] The MTA schedules public hearings at times and locations that accommodate as many community members as possible. Hearings are held in the evenings or mornings, Monday through Thursdays, and virtual attendance options are increasingly available for those who cannot attend in-person. The MTA notifies the public through digital platforms, printed announcements, and collaboration with community groups which helps increase awareness. Early notification and a variety of scheduling options make it easier for the public to engage with the MTA on important topics.
### [p026_c0008] Facilitating Public Input During Public Hearings
[p026_c0009] Public participation is encouraged during hearings via multiple options, including in-person comments, written submissions, and virtual attendance. During hearings, facilitators ensure that the environment is welcoming and structured to allow for respectful input. Speakers have two minutes to present their views, and MTA representatives at the dais listen, and do not respond to questions, they take notes on feedback for later review. Those who cannot attend are encouraged to submit written comments online ensuring that every voice is considered. Written comments are also accepted via postal mail for those who cannot attend in person or without internet access. Comments received by the posted deadline will be included in the public record.
[p026_c0010] The MTA creates the official public record for public hearings. The MTA Audio-Visual team records hearing proceedings. These recordings are then submitted to an approved transcription vendor, which prepares the official verbatim public hearing transcript. The transcript, together with all
[p026_c0011] _22_
[p026_c0012] _Master Page # 26 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 27

[p027_c0001] comments are received by the posted deadline, is compiled into the official public record. This record is distributed internally with a Staff Summary to the MTA Board Members and Executive Leadership for review and consideration as part of the decision-making process. The public record is maintained by the MTA in accordance with public access requirements.
### [p027_c0002] 5.4 Pre-Hearing Procedures
### [p027_c0003] Purchase Order Processing:
[p027_c0004] MTA GCR staff is required to initiate the processing of purchase orders for transcription, translations/interpreting services, and sign language interpreters for people with disabilities at least two months prior to the hearing.
### [p027_c0005] War Certificate [WAR = Willingness to Accept Risk]
[p027_c0006] The organizing agency gives a work order number or a WAR certificate which enables the MTA Police to receive reimbursement for protective services at the meeting or public hearing.
### [p027_c0007] Title VI Public Participation Checklist
[p027_c0008] To ensure compliance with Title VI of the Civil Rights Act, the MTA uses a Title VI Public Participation Checklist designed to promote full and fair engagement and prevent discrimination. This checklist includes specific outreach and engagement strategies to reach populations, particularly those who may be disproportionately impacted by MTA decisions.
[p027_c0009] Key checklist items include providing information in multiple languages, collaborating with community organizations that serve minority and low-income populations, and conducting targeted outreach in areas most affected by the proposed changes. Additionally, the check list involves reviewing participation rates and demographic data to ensure that all communities are adequately represented. By using the Title VI Checklist, (see the Appendix) the MTA fosters a public engagement process that is fair, and compliant with federal non-discrimination standards.
### [p027_c0010] Venue Selection/Site Survey:
[p027_c0011] All meeting sites must meet ADA accessibility standards (refer to the Accessibility Checklist for specific requirements). The meeting room should be accessible to people with mobility, hearing, and visual disabilities. For public hearings that generate significant public interest (e.g., fare and toll
[p027_c0012] _23_
[p027_c0013] _Master Page # 27 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 28

[p028_c0001] changes, congestion pricing, etc.), these selected sites must have a capacity of at least 250 people. Additionally, the hearing room should be free of barriers and have ample space to accommodate individuals with mobility, circulatory, visual, or respiratory disabilities who use various assistive devices such as walkers, canes, crutches, braces, manual or power wheelchairs, or electric scooters, all of which are primarily designed for people with disabilities. Site visits to evaluate spaces are conducted by: MTA GCR staff, MTA PD, and A/V Team.
### [p028_c0002] Securing Audio Visual Services:
[p028_c0003] MTA GCR staff are responsible for making sure all audio-visual services are in place for public meetings and hearings. When a meeting or hearing is held off-site, the MTA Bridges & Tunnels AV team provides full technical support. This includes video recording, multiple microphones with clear sound, and video monitors so both moderator and attendees can view presentations and Zoom feeds. The team also manages the video switcher used to transmit and livestream the Zoom feed through Headquarters and provides a visual and audible timer along with all necessary audio equipment. In cases where the meeting is held in the MTA's boardroom, the AV team at MTA Headquarters is engaged to provide their services.
### [p028_c0004] Certificate of Liability Insurance:
[p028_c0005] All off-site venues (non-MTA owned) require insurance. The coverage details and specified amounts will be provided by the venue and/or noted in the contract/agreement by the venue. A copy of the contract/agreement should be provided to the Risk and Insurance Management department three weeks before the hearing for the certificate.
### [p028_c0006] Meeting Schedule:
[p028_c0007] Meetings will be scheduled at times and in formats that prioritize convenience and accessibility for people with disabilities with both in-person and virtual participation options (hybrid).
### [p028_c0008] Meeting Formats:
[p028_c0009] Meetings and public hearings can be conducted in different formats, including in-person, hybrid, or virtual, depending on the size of the meeting group and specific requirements.
[p028_c0010] _24_
[p028_c0011] _Master Page # 28 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 29

### [p029_c0001] Timeliness of Hearing Commencement:
[p029_c0002] Every effort should be made to ensure that the hearing site is prepared and arranged in a manner that allows the hearing or meeting to begin precisely at the advertised starting time. MTA GCR is responsible for setting up the hearing site and managing all logistical aspects of the hearing.
### [p029_c0003] Catering services:
[p029_c0004] MTA GCR is responsible for arranging catering services for MTA Board members and staff at the hearing site. If the hearing takes place at a site represented by a food labor union; catering arrangement should be made through the union. Additionally, a separate room adjacent to the hearing room should be reserved for serving food for staff and MTA Police. A “green” room should also be secured for MTA Board members and MTA staff attending and or presenting at the hearing.
### [p029_c0005] Safety & Security
[p029_c0006] MTA GCR is responsible for scheduling MTA Police and/or building Security if the meeting/hearing is on the 20 th Floor board room, details of the meeting should be provided to the lead for building security. Hearing notices should be provided with MTA PD and building security for awareness.
### [p029_c0007] Designating a Hearing Officer
[p029_c0008] The Hearing Officer acts as the moderator for the public hearing. The appointee is a member of Government & Community Relations. Currently, Eminent Domain and Capital Projects hearings have both a Public Hearing Officer and a Moderator.
### [p029_c0009] Webpage, CRM and Hotline
[p029_c0010] MTA GCR coordinates with the various teams of the Creative and Customer Experience Teams to develop the registration and comment portals of the webpage and public hearing hotline.
### [p029_c0011] Opening the Registration Process
[p029_c0012] MTA Government & Community Relations (GCR) designates a member of the GCR operating agency staff to act as the registrar for people who wish to present oral testimony at the hearing. Registration takes place in an area that is accessible to people with disabilities. The registration area is equipped with a table and chairs and located near the entrance to the meeting room. The registration table contains materials explaining the proposal under consideration. Typical handouts are translated
[p029_c0013] _25_
[p029_c0014] _Master Page # 29 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 30

[p030_c0001] hearing notices in the top 9 non-English languages, large print, braille and any other printed materials including comment/suggestion cards. Hearing posters with multi-lingual QR codes and directional signage are made available in the building of the meeting space.
[p030_c0002] The registrar updates the coordination sheet of those registered to speak in-person or virtual. Virtual attendees who sign onto the Zoom waiting room are highlighted by the AV team or MTA GCR.
### [p030_c0003] 1. Opening the Hearing
[p030_c0004] Typically, the hearing officer is seated in the center of the dais, with the MTA Chair and CEO and agency president(s) and all members of the MTA Board who are present (subject matter experts are typically present for Fare and Toll hearings). The hearing officer calls the hearing to order by introducing the Safety Announcement (if in the boardroom), then proceeds by reading an opening statement that describes the purpose and procedures that govern the conduct of the hearing and instructions for auditory and visual cues for public speakers who are Blind or have Low Vision, Deaf or Hard of Hearing. After introducing the people seated at the dais, the hearing officer reads the hearing logistics and then the list of speakers in the order of registration alternating between in-person speakers and virtual attendees.
### [p030_c0005] 2. Calling the list of pre-registered Speakers
[p030_c0006] The hearing officer is seated at the dais with a laptop containing an Excel spreadsheet used to manage the hearing. The coordination sheet is a live, shared Excel document used by the registrar, AV team, and hearing officer to communicate and track active list of public speakers during the hearing. The hearing officer calls the names of pre-registered speakers (in-person and virtual) in an alternating order to balance in-person and virtual attendees. If preregistered speakers do not respond when the hearing officer calls them, or experience connectivity issues, their names are called again after all other preregistered speakers have been called.
### [p030_c0007] 3. Calling the List of In-Person Speakers
[p030_c0008] After the hearing officer has called the entire list of preregistered speakers, the hearing officer will then call the names of people who registered to speak at the hearing, The hearing remains open until all registered speakers in-person and virtual have testified or at the conclusion of the hearing as indicated on the legal notice.
[p030_c0009] _26_
[p030_c0010] _Master Page # 30 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 31

[p031_c0001] Note: Speakers may not transfer their speaking time to any other person. The hearing officer may insist that people speak only on the issue(s) under consideration at that hearing. If a group or organization registers to speak, the speaking time is still two minutes, and no additional time will be provided. Only one person will be allowed to speak from the group.
[p031_c0002] To the extent possible, the hearing should continue uninterrupted. If a break is necessary, the hearing officer should announce it ahead of time. The hearing officer should always announce the time before and after breaks, as well as at the conclusion of the meeting to ensure accurate documentation for the transcript.
### [p031_c0003] 4. Enforcing the Two Minute Speaking Limit
[p031_c0004] To accommodate all interested speakers, each speaker will be allotted two (2) minutes to address the subject matter of the hearing. An additional two minutes will be provided for individuals with a speech disability or those requiring ASL interpreter services. When a speaker approaches the podium/microphone and begins their testimony, the hearing officer activates the automated timer. For virtual attendees the Zoom timer is managed by the AV team, while the in-person timer is controlled by the hearing officer. The timer provides visual and auditory cues to notify the speaker when 30 seconds remain. At that point, the hearing officer requests the speaker to conclude their remarks.
### [p031_c0005] 5. Public Comment Limit: 120 Speakers or 240 Minutes
[p031_c0006] Public comment during fare and toll hearings should be limited to 240 minutes, or 4 hours per hearing or approximately 120 speakers. New speakers will be prioritized over individuals who have previously spoken at earlier hearings on the same public notice subject matter. Public comment begins at 6:00 pm and may extend to 10:00 pm. This is an estimate, and actual end time may vary based on scheduled breaks, speaker-no-shows, or deviations from the planned format or additional minutes provided to a person with a speech disability. Extended time to be used only with the speaker's request/consent.
[p031_c0007] On-site registration will close 30 minutes following the start of each hearing (if the cap has not been met). Registration should close once the 120-speaker capacity has been reached, this includes both virtual and in-person speakers.
[p031_c0008] The public hearing notice should include this public comment limitation.
[p031_c0009] Previous hearings using this format include the Fare hearings held in 2023 and 2025, as well as the CBDTP hearings.
[p031_c0010] _27_
[p031_c0011] _Master Page # 31 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 32

### [p032_c0001] 6. Maintaining Order/Ejection of Unruly Individuals
[p032_c0002] The hearing officer is responsible for maintaining an orderly proceeding and taking appropriate action to ensure the safety and comfort of the audience, MTA staff, and members of the Board. The hearing officer is authorized to suspend the hearing temporarily or permanently if a speaker refuses to end their testimony after the two-minute period has elapsed. The A/V staff may mute the microphone. The hearing officer may ask an MTA Police officer to escort an unruly speaker or member of the audience away from the speakers' microphone and/or eject the individual from the hearing room. If the speaker has joined virtually, they will be disconnected from Zoom.
### [p032_c0003] 7. Compiling Notes on Major Issues and Concerns
[p032_c0004] The hearing officer is responsible for taking note of all major issues and concerns presented at the hearing. Specific questions or issues regarding subject matters not covered by the hearing should be referred to the appropriate operating agency for follow-up. The hearing officer must keep detailed notes on issues raised during the testimony for inclusion in the hearing officer's report.
### [p032_c0005] 5.5 Post-Hearing Responsibilities
[p032_c0006] After a public hearing, the MTA has several responsibilities to ensure transparency, accountability, and responsiveness to public input. A key post-hearing responsibility is to compile and review all comments, creating a summary report that outlines the issues raised and provides responses or explanations where applicable. This report is typically compiled by the department.
### [p032_c0007] A. Writing and Submitting the Hearing Report
[p032_c0008] A summary of the hearing for distribution to the MTA Chair and CEO and/or the operating agency president(s) is prepared by the staff of the operating agency. The purpose of the report is to provide a consensus of the summary of the comments for the Chair and CEO and the agency presidents. The report should be submitted with written, online, and postal comments received regarding the hearing(s).
### [p032_c0009] B. Compiling, Reproducing, and Distributing Hearing Transcripts
[p032_c0010] Government & Community Relations (GCR) is responsible for receiving the original copy of the hearing transcript(s) from the transcription vendor in a timely fashion. The typical turnaround period for a public hearing transcript is three to five working days. The hearing officer must determine
[p032_c0011] _28_
[p032_c0012] _Master Page # 32 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 33

[p033_c0001] whether the transcripts are required on an expedited basis if it is necessary to advance the proposal in question to the MTA board for immediate action.
[p033_c0002] Upon receipt of the original transcript, GCR must conduct a complete and thorough review of its contents to assure the accurate transcription of the comments provided by participants at the hearing. After reviewing the transcript(s), copies of the hearing transcripts and written comments must also be provided to the MTA Board Office for distribution to the MTA Board.
### [p033_c0003] C. Processing Invoices
[p033_c0004] GCR are responsible for processing all invoices for site rental, food and beverages, transcripts/stenographic and interpreter services, and any miscellaneous expenses. While the Policy & External Relations Department pays the cost.
### [p033_c0005] Scheduling Hearings and Notifying the General Public
[p033_c0006] Scheduling and notifying the public are critical to maximizing participation and ensuring community members can contribute. The MTA considers various factors when scheduling hearings, including time of day, location accessibility, and potential conflict with other community events. Hearings are often scheduled during evenings (excluding holidays and Fridays), locations are chosen to be convenient for the affected community.
[p033_c0007] To notify the public, the MTA uses a multi-channel approach that includes publication of notices in general circulation newspapers, website updates, press releases, social media posts, and notices at key transit stations. These notifications are designed to reach a broad audience and target outreach to communities most affected by the issues under discussion. The MTA also works with local organizations, advocacy groups, and community leaders to spread awareness. Through this comprehensive approach to scheduling and notification, the MTA creates an accessible and welcoming environment for all.
[p033_c0008] _29_
[p033_c0009] _Master Page # 33 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 34

### [p034_c0001] ADA Event Checklist
[p034_c0002] Accessibility is a priority for all MTA public hearings and is guided by the ADA and the MTA's broader commitment to equal access and participation. An ADA Public Event Checklist is used to ensure that every hearing is fully accessible to people with disabilities, and accommodation may be requested in advance.
[p034_c0003] The checklist is designed to support compliance with applicable Federal and State accessible laws under the ADA and to guide event set up.
[p034_c0004] Note: If a meeting site is chosen that is not fully ADA accessible, written documentation must be prepared explaining the reason for the site selection and the steps taken to provide reasonable accommodation for accessibility. This documentation must be maintained in MTA GCR files and shared with the Department of Diversity & Civil Rights (DDCR).
[p034_c0005] _30_
[p034_c0006] _Master Page # 34 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_
