The following data reflects those assessments during this reporting period.

### [p298_c0009] BUS SURVEYS
[p298_c0010] The Fall 2024 bi-annual Customer Count survey was conducted October 14 – 31, 2024, with English and Spanish offered online and by phone. MTA received responses for more than 79,000 subway lines, 24,000 bus routes and 3,500 AAR evaluations from over 40,000 MTA Bus Company and NYCT customers.
[p298_c0011] The Spring 2025 bi-annual Customer Count survey was conducted April 21- May 4, 2025, and was offered online, in nine languages and on the phone in English and Spanish. MTA received responses for more than 90,000 subway lines, 46,000 bus routes and 5,000 AAR evaluations from over 80,000 MTA Bus

## Page 299

[p299_c0001] Company and NYCT customers. Our sample is weighted by ridership, and relevant respondent demographics using ACS 1-Year 2023 Estimates
### [p299_c0002] WRITTEN TRANSLATIONS
[p299_c0003] NYCT and MTA Bus Company has translated written vital information on service and fare changes, safety/security notices, schedules, maps, emergency evacuation procedures and construction projects into Spanish and other languages frequently impacted by their programs, services or activities. Information is also publicized on the MTA website and can be translated into several languages using Google automated translation program. Universal pictograms or pictographs are used for translation of emergency evacuation and safety/security notices. Interpreters are provided at public hearings or meetings and notices are published in a variety of newspapers, including El Diario, in connection with environmental procedures, real estate acquisitions, and other construction projects.
### [p299_c0004] COMMUNITY BOARDS AND ORGANIZATIONS
[p299_c0005] NYCT and MTA Bus Company greatly value the input of all its customers, and undertakes extensive efforts to ensure that minority, low-income and limited English proficiency LEP individuals can offer their perspective and opinion. Both agencies hold public meetings to keep area residents informed of transportation decisions and/or changes and invite their comments. Communication may be general at times or targeted to a specific audience. Meetings are scheduled at locations throughout NYCT and MTA Bus Company's operating system to ensure that a broad range of the customer base can hear and be heard on issues that affect them.
### [p299_c0006] FACTOR 3. THE NATURE AND IMPORTANCE OF THE SERVICES PROVIDED BY MTA'S PROGRAM.
[p299_c0007] The MTA recognizes that its services remain an integral core of living within the city, and that the inability of LEP persons to effectively utilize public transit (due to a language barrier) could adversely affect a rider's ability to access health care, childcare, education, or employment opportunities within the service area.
[p299_c0008] The MTA Police Department (MTAPD) also recognizes the importance of effective and accurate communication between its officers and the LEP community they serve. Language barriers can sometimes inhibit or even prohibit LEP individuals from accessing and/or understanding important rights, obligations, in difficult or emergent situations. MTAPD has established guidelines for its members to follow when securing interpreters or interpreter services during interactions with customers with limited English proficiency.
### [p299_c0009] FACTOR 4. THE RESOURCES AVAILABLE TO THE MTA AND THE OVERALL COSTS
[p299_c0010] The MTA is committed to ensuring that LEP individuals can meaningfully participate in all programs and services. Each MTA Agency has developed and implemented agency-specific LAPs. The MTA provides language services to the LEP community that are sufficient and reasonable, to afford equitable access to all programs, activities and services, when practical and within the scope of available funding. An Agency-Wide Vital Documents Translations List has been created to ensure consistent service of the LEP community. (Attachment 7a)
[p299_c0011] _Master Page # 299 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_
[p299_c0012] _Page 26 of 30_

## Page 300

[p300_c0001] Due to MTA's financial constraints, full translation of all transportation documents is not feasible. Based on the Safe Harbor Provision guidelines detailed within FTA Circular 4702.1B, The MTA will weigh the costs and benefits of translating vital documents for potential LEP groups, considering the expense of translating the documents, the barriers to meaningful translation or interpretation of technical transit information, the likelihood of frequent changes in documents, the existence of multiple dialects within a single language group, the apparent literacy rate in an LEP group, and other relevant factors.
[p300_c0002] As outlined under the Safe Harbor Provisions, when there are more than 50 persons in a language group that reaches the 5% trigger, the MTA will translate vital written materials. If there are fewer than 50 persons in a language group that reaches the 5% trigger, the MTA will provide written notice, in the primary language of the LEP group, of the right to receive competent oral interpretation of free written materials.
[p300_c0003] The MTA will undertake this examination when an eligible LEP group constitutes 5% of an eligible client group (for example, 5% of MTA Agencies' service area) or 1,000 persons, whichever is less, of the total population of persons eligible to be served or likely to be affected or encountered. The MTA considers the resources available, the demographics, frequency, and importance of contact in determining the type and extent of language services provided and will continue to explore means of delivering competent and accurate language services in as cost-effective manner as possible.
### [p300_c0004] VI. MTA AGENCIES' IMPLEMENTATION PLAN
[p300_c0005] The MTA uses various methods of communication to ensure that the LEP population is provided with vital and accurate information in a timely manner. This communication occurs both in the form of oral communication with the MTA staff and written informational materials. The MTA contracts with an outside vendor to provide oral translation services free of cost to customers.
[p300_c0006] MTA translates written vital documents on service and fare changes, safety/security notices, schedules, maps, emergency evacuation procedures and construction projects into other languages of customers that frequently use our service. Universal pictograms or pictographs are used for translation of emergency evacuation and safety/security notices. Vital information is also publicized and translated on the MTA website.
[p300_c0007] Interpreters are provided at public hearings or meetings and notices in connection with environmental procedures, real estate acquisitions, and construction projects are published in a variety of non-English speaking newspapers, including El Especialito, Sing Tao and World Journal (Chinese).
[p300_c0008] Below is an abbreviated outline of the steps the MTA takes to implement LEP assistance.
[p300_c0009] 1) Identifying LEP persons who need language assistance - As described in detail above, the MTA identifies LEP populations based on various sources, including data from the U.S. Census Bureau, and by gathering information from the LEP community.
[p300_c0010] 2) Providing language assistance
[p300_c0011] a. Oral Language Assistance Services - Oral communications between the MTA and its beneficiaries often are an integral part in the exchange of information. In general, the MTA will provide this assistance through the following:
[p300_c0012] _Master Page # 300 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_
[p300_c0013] _Page 27 of 30_

## Page 301

- [p301_c0001] • Language translation services, through contracted vendor Propio, provided free of cost. Customer Service representatives are trained to contact Propio when assisting a LEP caller. • Language translation services, through a GCR contracted vendor Propio, provided free of cost. Customer Service representatives are trained to contact Propio when assisting a LEP caller. • Other MTA Agencies employees are advised to request assistance from Agency bilingual staff or transfer all LEP calls to the Customer Service Department. • MTAPD officers – When assisting LEP individuals, officers are trained to contact the following: ○ Language Line, (Propio, Inc.) ○ Member of the MTAPD who is bilingual. ○ Family member or friend of the LEP individual and/or bystanders — only for informal, non-confrontational situations and only to obtain basic information at the request of the LEP individual. ○ Bilingual Sworn officer from an associated law enforcement agency. ○ Civilian bilingual employee of the MTAPD.
[p301_c0002] b. Written Language Services - The MTA, through a GCR contracted vendor, provides written translation of many documents including, customer rights and the complaint process, service and fare changes, safety/security and emergency information, schedules, maps, and construction project notices and information. The translated languages will include Spanish, as the top non-English language spoken throughout the entire MTA service area, and other languages depending on the impacted service area.
- [p301_c0003] • The MTA will provide translations of public hearing notices, service or fare changes, and when needed, written responses to questions from the public. • Information is publicized on MTA’s website and can be translated into several languages using the Google automated translation program. • Universal pictograms or pictographs are used for translation of emergency evacuation and safety/security information on buses, subways and commuter railroads. • Title VI Notice to the Public is posted in the top nine, non-English languages of the MTA service area on the MTA website and displayed in stations and buses in other languages depending on the area. • “Filing a Title VI Complaint” can be found on the MTA website translated into the top nine languages of the MTA service area. The notice can also be translated into several languages using Google Translate, the automated translation service. • Customer surveys are translated, at a minimum, into Spanish.
[p301_c0004] _Master Page # 301 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_
[p301_c0005] _Page 28 of 30_

## Page 302

- [p302_c0001] • MetroCard/OMNY (Tap and Ride) Vending Machines are in almost every station. Currently the languages available on these machines include English, Spanish, Chinese, and Italian. The available languages reflect the language needs of the community. • MYmta for iOS and Android users offers regionwide travel support, including real-time arrival and schedule information for subway, bus, Long Island Rail Road, and Metro-North service is provided in English, Spanish, French, Korean, Russian, and simplified Chinese.
[p302_c0002] 3) Training - The MTA affiliated and subsidiary agencies are responsible for implementing Agency-specific Title VI/LEP training programs for their front-line employees. Typical components of training may include, but will not be limited to the following:
- [p302_c0003] • Understanding Title VI/LEP responsibilities. • Background on LEP populations within the service area. • Description of the type of language assistance MTA currently provides. • Instructions on how to assist a LEP Customer. • Procedure for handling a Title VI complaint
[p302_c0004] 4) Providing Notice to LEP Persons - The MTA has established the following methods to inform LEP individuals, Community Boards, and the general public, of available no-fee LEP services:
- [p302_c0005] • A LEP notice has been posted on the MTA website at www.mta.info notifying visitors of the availability of language assistance at no cost to the user. The notice has been translated into the top nine non-English languages of the MTA service area and can be translated into more than 100 additional languages using the Google automated translation service. • Title VI complaint procedures and a summary of MTA's Title VI/LAP Plan are posted on the MTA website at www.mta.info notifying visitors of their right to file a Title VI complaint (which includes LEP). • Information on service changes, fares, safety/security notices, schedules, maps, emergency evacuation procedures and construction project notices translated into Spanish or other languages directly impacted by its programs, services or activities.
[p302_c0006] 5) Monitoring and Updating the LAP - The MTA LAP has been designed to be flexible and can be updated as needed. Revisions to the plan will be conducted at a minimum of every three years and will be based upon the cumulative results of annual assessments.
[p302_c0007] Each update will include, but will not be limited to a review of the following information:
- [p302_c0008] • Most recent LEP demographics of the population of MTA Agencies' service area; • Cost and use of language services in particular languages, both interpretation and translation; • Current communication needs of LEP communities;
[p302_c0009] _Master Page # 302 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_
[p302_c0010] _Page 29 of 30_

## Page 303

- [p303_c0001] • Effectiveness of the current LAP Plan; • Number and types of customer complaints about the LAP Plan and how they are handled, the outcome and follow-up action taken; and • Periodic feedback from the Community Boards about the effectiveness of the LAP Plan.
### [p303_c0002] VII. DISSEMINATION OF THE MTA’S LAP
[p303_c0003] The MTA’s LAP and Title VI Program will be disseminated in English and other languages upon request by contacting the Department of Diversity and Civil Rights. A summary of MTA Agencies’ LAP will be shared with Community Boards and minority organizations to inform them of the various forms of language assistance available.
### [p303_c0004] VIII. LEP COMPLAINT PROCEDURES
[p303_c0005] Complaints of discrimination involving LEP, Title VI, and related statutes will be investigated under the All Agency Policy Directive, Title VI and Related Nondiscrimination Laws Complaint Policy Management. MTA’s Title VI and Related Nondiscrimination policy statement can be found here on the MTA website: Title VI Policy . Questions or comments regarding this LAP may be directed to:
[p303_c0006] Chief Diversity and Inclusion Officer Department of Diversity and Civil Rights 2 Broadway, 16th Floor New York, NY 10004-2207 646-252-1385

### [p307_c0001] Title VI - LIRR Capital Projects - Period Status - 2020 - 2024

## Page 328

### [p328_c0001] AGENCY PLANS
### [p328_c0002] NEW YORK CITY TRANSIT
### [p328_c0003] Overview
[p328_c0004] New York City Transit (NYCT), together with Staten Island Railway (SIR) and MTA Bus, operate the busiest and most extensive subway and bus systems in the United States. The NYCT system is the core of the MTA's regional network, and, in New York City, a majority of households do not own a car. In 2023, 1.58 billion rides were taken on NYCT services. Much of the system is over 100 years old and operates 24 hours a day, 365 days a year. Our trains, buses, stations, and supporting infrastructure—like track, signals, power systems, rail yards, and bus depots—are the foundation of our network. These assets require substantial and sustained capital investment to address historical underinvestment and to allow us to deliver the frequent and reliable service our riders expect.
### [p328_c0005] Proposed 2025-2029 Capital Plan - $47.8 billion
[p328_c0006]

```text
Category Proposed Budget ($in millions) Percent Subway Cars $7,617 16% Buses $3,293 7% Passenger Stations, including Accessibility $11,931 25% Track $3,717 8% Line Equipment $732 2% Line Structures $5,636 12% Signals and Communications $6,907 15% Traction Power $2,996 6% Shops and Yards $1,647 3% Bus Depots $370 1% Service Vehicles $613 1% Miscellaneous (Facilities and Program Support) $1,583 3% Staten Island Railway $344 1% Subtotal NYCT & SIR $47,386 100% MTA Bus $454 Total $47,840
```
[p328_c0007] All counts include NYCT, SIR, and MTA Bus assets
[p328_c0008] 6,787 Subway Cars
[p328_c0009] 5,840 Buses
[p328_c0010] 38 Bus Depots & Facilities
[p328_c0011] 493 Stations
[p328_c0012] 326 Elevators
[p328_c0013] 231 Escalators
[p328_c0014] 266 Miles of Line Structures
[p328_c0015] 694 Miles of Mainline Track
[p328_c0016] 794 Miles of Signal Equipment
[p328_c0017] 217 Signal Interlockings
[p328_c0018] 233 Substations
[p328_c0019] 75 Shops and Yards
[p328_c0020] 209 Fan Plants
[p328_c0021] 679 Work Train Cars

## Page 329

### [p329_c0001] AGENCY PLANS
### [p329_c0002] BUDGET TABLES
[p329_c0003] The Budget Tables are the complete list of capital investment projects to be initiated over the next 5 years, provided in tabular format. The New York City Transit example below explains all of the information that is included in a typical budget table.
> [p329_c0004] 1 The Agency name 2 The Category name 3 The Agency (T), Capital Plan (9), and Category (09) portions of the ACEP code 4 The Element name and Element portion of the ACEP code 5 The Project description and Project portion of the ACEP code 6 The Needs Code . Separate from the ACEP Code, the Needs Code indicates the focus of each project: State of Good Repair (SGR) projects achieve a state of good repair by renewing assets that have surpassed their useful lives Normal Replacement (NR) projects preserve a state of good repair by renewing assets that are near the end of their useful lives System Improvement (SI) projects enhance the network by providing both new capabilities and better customer experience Network Expansion (NE) projects extend the reach of the MTA network by expanding the service offering Various (VAR) projects include multiple needs codes in one project 7 The Commitments columns indicate the share of the project budget that is planned to be committed in each year of the 2025-2029 five-year period
[p329_c0005]

### [p337_c0008] Purpose:
[p337_c0009] To obtain Board adoption of the annexed updated Policies under Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 ("Title VI Policies"), governing MTA New York City Transit ("NYC Transit"), MTA Bus Company ("MTA Bus"), MTA Long Island Rail Road ("LIRR") and MTA Metro-North Railroad ("MNR"), in satisfaction of FTA requirements set forth in its revised Title VI Circular.
### [p337_c0010] Discussion:
[p337_c0011] The Federal Transit Administration ("FTA") has issued a revised Title VI Circular (Circular 4702.1B, effective October 1, 2012), to assist its grantees in complying with their obligations under Title VI. The FTA Circular requires, among other things, that transportation agencies update, and post for public comment, a set of Title VI policies as part of their Title VI Programs. Pursuant to the requirements of the Circular, the MTA's operating agencies have reviewed and updated their existing Title VI policies, which include existing policies for evaluation of fare and service changes as well as existing system-wide service standards and service policies.
[p337_c0012] Consistent with the Circular's guidance, the updated Title VI Policies have been organized by mode of service. The attachment thus contains a set of Title VI Policies for commuter rail service provided by MTA LIRR and MTA MNR and a parallel set of Title VI Policies for subway and bus services provided by MTA NYC Transit and MTA Bus. The policies, now organized in the manner conforming to the new Circular, remain substantially the same in content as the pre-existing Title VI policies of the agencies.
[p337_c0013] In accordance with the public input requirements of the FTA Circular, MTA posted the proposed updated Title VI Policies on MTA's website, inviting comment. As of May 24, 2013, no comments of a substantive nature had been received. MTA and agency representatives also held a focus group meeting on May 22, 2013, with transportation advocacy and community groups, at which the updated Title VI Policies were reviewed.
### [p337_c0014] Recommendation:
[p337_c0015] It is recommended that the Board adopt the annexed Title VI Policies, in satisfaction of the requirements of the FTA Title VI Circular.
[p337_c0016] _Master Page # 337 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 338

### [p338_c0001] BOARD RESOLUTION
[p338_c0002] WHEREAS, in a Staff Summary, dated June 3, 2013, the Department of Diversity and Civil Rights has recommended that the Board adopt updated policies under Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 ("Title VI"), governing MTA New York City Transit, MTA Bus Company, MTA Long Island Rail Road and MTA Metro-North Railroad, in satisfaction of Federal Transit Administration ("FTA") requirements set forth in its revised Title VI Circular;
[p338_c0003] WHEREAS, the proposed policies attached to the Staff Summary incorporate existing policies for the evaluation of fare and service changes as well as existing system-wide service standards and service policies;
[p338_c0004] WHEREAS, in accordance with the FTA Title VI Circular, the proposed policies were posted on MTA's website, inviting comments, and a meeting was held on May 22, 2013, with transportation advocacy groups, at which the updated Title VI policies were reviewed;
[p338_c0005] NOW, THEREFORE IT IS RESOLVED that the Board adopts the Title VI Policies described in and attached to said Staff Summary.
[p338_c0006] _Master Page # 338 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 340

### [p340_c0001] New York City Transit
### [p340_c0002] Bus
### [p340_c0003] MTA New York City Transit and MTA Bus Company Major Service and Fare Change Policy, and System-wide Service Standards and Policies
[p340_c0004] In accordance with Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 (Title VI), MTA New York City Transit (NYCT) 1 and MTA Bus Company (MTA Bus) have established their Major Service and Fare Change Policy, and System-wide Service Standards and Policies for each mode of service that is provided.
### [p340_c0005] I. MTA New York City Transit and MTA Bus Company Major Service and Fare Change Policy
### [p340_c0006] 1. Major Service Change
[p340_c0007] Prior to conducting a service change analysis under Title VI, NYCT and MTA Bus first determine whether a particular service change is considered to be major or minor. 2 If a service change is determined to be “major,” a Title VI analysis is conducted in order to determine whether the major service change will have a disparate impact or result in a disproportionate burden on any Title VI or Environmental Justice (“EJ”) protected class.
[p340_c0008] NYCT and MTA Bus use the following definitions of “major” service changes:
- [p340_c0009] • Route restructuring actions resulting in at least a 25% change in overall route length; • Service frequency changes resulting in at least a 25% change in annual revenue vehicle miles (“RVM”) Annual RVMs are a compilation of Weekday, Saturday and Sunday RVMs; or • Span change actions resulting in at least one hour change in service span.
[p340_c0010] The following exceptions to the definition of “major” service changes apply:
- [p340_c0011] • Demonstration projects or experimental service; • Standard seasonal or holiday adjustments; • Temporary schedule changes to enable performance of line maintenance or capital improvement work; or • Temporary changes in response to emergency situations, service disruptions or events beyond the control of NYCT or MTA Bus.
### [p340_c0012] 2. Fare Changes
[p340_c0013] NYCT and MTA Bus require a Title VI fare equity analysis to be performed for any proposed fare change presented to the Board.
[p340_c0014] _1 For purposes of these standards and policies, NYCT refers to the New York City Transit Authority and its statutory subsidiary, Manhattan and Bronx Transit Operating Authority (MaBSTOA)._
[p340_c0015] _2 Multiple changes implemented concurrently as a single related action will be reviewed as a single change for purposes of determining whether such change is major._
[p340_c0016] _Page 1 of 8_
[p340_c0017] _Master Page # 340 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 341

### [p341_c0001] New York City Transit
### [p341_c0002] Bus
### [p341_c0003] 3. Disparate Impact/Disproportionate Burden Policy
### [p341_c0004] Adverse Effects
[p341_c0005] NYCT and MTA Bus perform statistical analyses for minority/non-minority and for at or below poverty/above poverty groups to assess if proposed changes may disproportionately affect any of the protected classes in terms of average trip cost, travel time or passenger loading (for Major Service Changes) or average fare media cost (for Fare Changes).
### [p341_c0006] Threshold
[p341_c0007] Using a statistical test (t-test and/or Chi-square, where appropriate), NYCTA and MTA Bus will apply a 95 +/- 5% confidence level to ascertain if there are significant differences between any of the groups being compared, using results from the impact analysis.
- [p341_c0008] • If the result falls within the boundaries of the test statistic, there is no "statistically significant" difference between the groups and no further analysis is necessary. • If the result falls outside the test statistic there is a "statistically significant difference" between the groups associated with the service change. It then must be determined if the difference represents an adverse impact to any protected class (e.g., an increase in average travel time for minorities for a service reduction represents an adverse impact). • Any adverse impact will result in a consideration of an alteration to the service change and/or implementation of mitigation measures, or, where neither alteration nor mitigation is possible, an explanation for the adverse impact and/or justification for the service change.
### [p341_c0009] II. MTA New York City Transit and MTA Bus Company System-wide Service Standards
[p341_c0010] The following fixed route modes are provided by NYCT: Subway, Local Bus and Express Bus. MTA Bus provides Local Bus and Express Bus modes.
### [p341_c0011] 1. Service Availability
[p341_c0012] Service availability is a general measure of the distribution of routes within a transit system. NYCT and MTA Bus use differing measures as a result of their dissimilar service area densities and operational characteristics. NYCT Bus services are designed to be complementary with NYCT Subway services, providing feeder routes to many subway locations, which are part of a fixed infrastructure, allowing riders to transfer easily between modes to complete their trips. Therefore, NYCT Service Availability includes both bus and subway stops.
[p341_c0013] The NYCT service area comprises the five counties of New York City: New York, Queens, Bronx, Kings and Richmond, and provides high density local, express and select bus routes and subway lines in a complimentary service pattern. The MTA Bus service area comprises the counties of Bronx, Kings, Nassau, New York, Queens and Westchester, providing express and local bus service.
[p341_c0014] _Page 2 of 8_
[p341_c0015] _Master Page # 341 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 342

### [p342_c0001] New York City Transit
### [p342_c0002] NYCT (Bus & Subway)
[p342_c0003] NYCT measures Service Availability by the distance that a person must travel to gain access to its (Bus stop or Subway station) services. The distance to transit service is measured by the distance from the geographic center of a given Census tract to the closest available service. NYCT aims to provide transit service within ¼ mile from the geographic center of a given Census tract (or Centroid). Factors governing bus stop placement include route spacing, land use, population density and the location of major traffic generators, and type of bus service (local, limited or express).
### [p342_c0004] MTA Bus
[p342_c0005] MTA Bus measures Service Availability by the distribution of express and local routes within the MTA Bus service area, which includes Bus Stop Spacing. MTA Bus uses different criteria for express service and local service bus stop spacing, pursuant to the “Bus Stop Spacing Guidelines.” Local bus routes have minimum desired spacing interval of approximately 750 feet or about three blocks between bus stops while express bus routes have a minimum desired spacing interval of approximately 1,300 feet or about five blocks between bus stops.
### [p342_c0006] 2. On-Time Performance
[p342_c0007] On-Time Performance is a measure of trips completed as scheduled. Several criteria may define what is considered to be “on time.”
### [p342_c0008] NYCT (Bus & Subway Wait Assessment)
[p342_c0009] NYCT’s measurement of On-Time Performance is called “Wait Assessment.” “Wait Assessment” is measured on bus routes with the highest passenger volume within each borough (plus eight associated limited services and two +SelectBus routes) and for all Subway routes, as part of the Performance Indicator (“PI”) program.
[p342_c0010] Wait Assessment measures whether scheduled headways are maintained during the hours of most frequent service and is defined as the number of service intervals that are no more than the scheduled interval plus the following:
[p342_c0011] Local Bus 7 am to midnight
[p342_c0012] Acceptable Interval in Excess of Scheduled Interval
[p342_c0013] + 3 Minutes (Peak), + 5 Minutes (Off-Peak)
[p342_c0014] Peak hours are 7 am - 9 am and 4 pm - 7 pm
[p342_c0015] Off-Peak hours are 9 am - 4 pm and 7 pm - 12 am
[p342_c0016] Subway 6 am to midnight
[p342_c0017] Acceptable Interval in Excess of Scheduled Interval
[p342_c0018] + 25% of scheduled interval (6 am- midnight)
[p342_c0019] _Page 3 of 8_
[p342_c0020] _Master Page # 342 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 343

### [p343_c0001] New York City Transit
### [p343_c0002] Bus
[p343_c0003] Routes may be added or deleted to the program as required (i.e., a new +SelectBus route is started).
### [p343_c0004] MTA Bus (On-Time Performance)
[p343_c0005] MTA Bus uses the following methodology to measure On-Time Performance. On-Time Performance is presently assessed by MTA Bus by the use of traffic checks of the bus trips that either leave an enroute location or arrive at a terminal in accord with the public schedule. The times that the buses leave timepoint locations are then tabulated. These tabulations result in a measure of the On-Time Performance.
[p343_c0006] The parameters for On-Time Performance assessment require that each bus trip of a particular route must not be earlier than one minute before or not be later than five minutes after its scheduled departure time at each of its assessed (terminal or enroute) time points.
### [p343_c0007] 3. Vehicle Headway
[p343_c0008] Vehicle Headway is the time interval between two vehicles, traveling in the same direction on the same route.
### [p343_c0009] Subway
[p343_c0010] The Subway headway, expressed in minutes, indicates the average maximum amount of time that a person should spend waiting for a train during the analyzed time period. Shorter headways, with less waiting time, are indicative of "better" service provision. Average headways are taken from official and authorized subway timetables, and display the average scheduled interval between trains (in minutes) for a given route at a specific time-point location. If there are a large number of riders, then the headway is short since more frequent service is needed to maintain guideline loading levels. Although headways are route specific, more than one service may run on a given set of tracks, at certain locations, thus decreasing overall wait times.
[p343_c0011] NYCT subway headways are based on publicly approved Service Guidelines, which specify "policy," or minimum service headways, which are 10 minutes (Peak, Weekday Mid-day and Saturday Mid-day), 12 minutes (Weekday Evening, Saturday Evening and Sunday) and 20 minutes (Overnight) so that even during low ridership hours, transit riders have an expectation that a fixed level of service will be maintained.
### [p343_c0012] Bus
[p343_c0013] The parameters for setting bus headways for local and express services are based on Board approved system-wide loading guidelines. If there are a large number of riders, then the headway is short since required trips must be more frequent. The guidelines also specify a "policy," or minimum service headway, so that even during low ridership hours, transit riders have an expectation that a fixed level of service will be maintained. The guideline used by NYCT and MTA Bus is based on the volume of ridership passing maximum load points ("MLPs"), with
[p343_c0014] _Page 4 of 8_
[p343_c0015] _Master Page # 343 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 344

### [p344_c0001] New York City Transit
### [p344_c0002] Bus
[p344_c0003] lessening thresholds of service (longer headways) when loads do not justify more frequent service. The guideline varies by type of service (express and local) and size of bus (45-foot express, 40-foot standard local, 60-foot articulated local). We schedule for up to a maximum of a seated load on express buses at all times, up to a maximum of a seated load on local buses during off-peak periods, and up to a maximum of approximately 50% standees in addition to seated load on local buses during peak periods.
### [p344_c0004] 4. Vehicle Load
[p344_c0005] Vehicle loading standards are established by Board-approved passenger loading guidelines, which mandate acceptable maximum vehicle capacities. NYCT and MTA Bus use the average load factor for conducting evaluations under Title VI.
### [p344_c0006] Subway
[p344_c0007] For Subway travel, most passengers travel toward the Central Business District ("CBD") in the a.m. rush period, and away from the CBD during the p.m. rush period. Ridership is generally lower and spread over a longer period of time in the p.m. rush period. As a result, ridership levels at the MLP for most routes will be lower during the p.m. rush period than the a.m. rush period.
[p344_c0008] The load factor is the average passengers per car divided by the scheduled load. The number of passengers per car ("Average/Car") is the number of passengers divided by the number of cars, with the result rounded to the nearest whole number. The scheduled load is the Board-approved loading guideline for the class of cars in service. Subway loading guidelines differ for the three sizes of subway cars used by NYC Transit: "A" Division 51-foot cars (car classes R62, R62A, R142, and R142A), "B" Division 60-foot cars (car classes R32, R42, R143, R160A, and R160B), and "B" Division 75-foot cars (car classes R46, R68, and R68A). All subway service is scheduled to provide for standees during peak periods. When service operates more frequently, the guidelines allow more customers per car. The maximum capacity for each car size is based on a standing space of three square feet per standing customer. The number of allowable peak period riders per "A" Division car is 110. Similarly the numbers of allowable peak period riders per "B" Division cars are 145 and 175, for 60-foot and 75-foot cars, respectively.
[p344_c0009] All subway service is scheduled up to 125 percent of a seated load during off-peak periods.
[p344_c0010] A factor of less than one (1.0) indicates that the average load is below the loading guidelines. In cases where factors are greater than one (1.0), the fixed rail infrastructure and safe distance vehicle operation may prevent adding service on lines running at maximum operational levels.
### [p344_c0011] Bus
[p344_c0012] The guideline for average riders per trip for local and express bus services is based on official system-wide loading guidelines, previously approved by the Board. These loading guidelines vary between different times of day/week, bus sizes and bus route configurations (grid vs. feeder). A feeder route takes most riders to the same final destination in the morning, and takes them away from that point in the evening. The destination is often a transfer point such as a ferry terminal or a subway station. The MLP for a feeder route is usually at the destination point
[p344_c0013] _Page 5 of 8_
[p344_c0014] _Master Page # 344 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 345

### [p345_c0001] New York City Transit
### [p345_c0002] Bus
[p345_c0003] or at a stop very near the end, in the a.m. period, and at the same point, which becomes the new origin, in the p.m. period. In contrast, a grid route picks up and discharges passengers throughout the entire route, in both directions. The MLP of a grid route tends to be near the center of the route in both the a.m. and the p.m. periods.
[p345_c0004] The average load factor is the average riders per trip divided by the number of bus seats. The average number of seats per bus for Standard size, Coach, and Articulated buses are 40, 55 and 62, respectively. Therefore, a factor that is less than one (1.0) indicates that the actual loads are below established guidelines at the MLP point.
### [p345_c0005] III. MTA New York City Transit and MTA Bus Company System-Wide Service Policies
[p345_c0006] NYCT and MTA Bus apply the following Policies for System-wide Policy Indicators, based on the NYCT Service Guidelines Manual, the Department of Subways ("DOS") Rail Fleet Management Plan and the Department of Buses ("DOB") Program Standards. The following fixed route modes are provided by NYCT: Subway, Local Bus and Express Bus. MTA Bus provides Local Bus and Express Bus modes.
### [p345_c0007] 1. Vehicle Assignment
[p345_c0008] Vehicle Assignment refers to the process by which transit vehicles are placed into service in depots and on routes throughout a transit system.
### [p345_c0009] Subway
[p345_c0010] The assignment of subway cars to individual subway routes is guided by the practices outlined in the Rail Fleet Management Plan. As detailed in the Rail Fleet Management Plan, car assignments are based on several factors, which include ridership levels on each subway route, operational factors such as subway clearance and car class compatibility, and maintenance factors, such as the location of repair and overhaul facilities.
[p345_c0011] Ridership concerns, such as severe overcrowding, can lead to major capital investments to increase capacity, such as the purchase of additional subway cars or the construction of new subway lines like the Second Avenue Subway. Maintenance and operational factors are important as the NYCT fleet is not uniform and no single car class can operate in passenger service on all subway routes. NYCT Subways has two physically separate divisions with different gauges and, consequently, different car sizes. Cars and trains on the A Division (numbered routes 1 through 7, plus the S 42nd Street Shuttle) are physically smaller than those on the B Division. Signal system needs may affect car assignments and will become increasingly important as NYCT modernizes with Communications-Based Train Control ("CBTC"). New technology cars delivered since 2000 are all CBTC-compatible.
[p345_c0012] Subway cars are maintained on a daily basis at 13 car maintenance shops throughout the system, supported by two heavy overhaul shops, all of which handle specific car classes. As a result of the ridership, operational, and maintenance considerations outlined above, certain car classes tend to cluster at specific shops and, hence, are assigned to specific routes. Car assignments for individual routes and shops also tend to remain fairly steady for years, and sometimes decades,
[p345_c0013] _Page 6 of 8_
[p345_c0014] _Master Page # 345 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 346

### [p346_c0001] New York City Transit
### [p346_c0002] Bus
[p346_c0003] except when new fleets are replacing older fleets. Therefore, there is no applicable standard or guideline for subway fleet age.
### [p346_c0004] Bus
[p346_c0005] NYCT and MTA Bus seek to maintain a uniform fleet age across the system, with the average fleet age standard for any depot or division between 6 and 7 ½ years. DOB has developed extensive supporting policies to facilitate compliance with this standard.
[p346_c0006] New buses are assigned to various depots in the system based on the needs of the individual depot, not the needs of the individual route. The assignment of vehicles is depot based, not route based. However, express buses are assigned only to express routes. Depot assignment is predicated on a number of independent and interrelated factors, such as peak service requirements by mode, uniform fleet age and alternative fuel types.
[p346_c0007] The depots with the oldest buses will in general get the greatest share of any new buses. However, in order to properly maintain buses at each location, it is necessary that a limited variety of buses be assigned to each depot. This enables mechanics at a given location to be familiar with a variety of buses and minimizes inventory needs for repair parts. Specific equipment assignments for a route are not made within the depot; each route is served with the first bus available. The average bus age is independent of a particular route in a depot, given that bus assignments are not route specific.
### [p346_c0008] 2. Transit Amenities
[p346_c0009] Transit amenities refer to items of comfort and convenience available to the general riding public.
### [p346_c0010] Subway
[p346_c0011] The transit amenities and corresponding standards listed below apply to Subway stations. The following measures are used to evaluate subway transit amenities:
- [p346_c0012] ▪ Benches - Benches are placed in paid areas on mezzanines or at platform level. ▪ Trash Receptacles - Trash receptacles are to be placed near seating benches and on platforms. Final locations will be based on the recommendations of the maintenance unit of the Stations Department. ▪ Train Arrival Annunciator - Every station, where the (24 hours) fare control area is on a different level from the train platform, has at least one train arrival annunciator to alert passengers with audio and/or visual components of impending train arrivals and to direct them to the proper platform. ▪ MetroCard Vending Machines ("MVM") - Space permitting, there should be least two MVMs per entry control area (excluding High Entry and Exit Turnstile locations within a station) selling fare media with multilingual capabilities. Where space is not available for two MVMs, at least one MVM should be provided. ▪ Passenger Information Center ("PIC") - PIC displays shall be installed in the unpaid zone of every full-time control area; installation depends on the availability of wall space; installation in part-time control areas depends on the

### [p347_c0001] New York City Transit
### [p347_c0002] Bus
[p347_c0003] display's vulnerability to vandalism. Where available wall space does not permit complete installation of the PIC display, the items below should be displayed in order of priority: 1. Guide-A-Ride Maps 2. System Map 3. Neighborhood Map 4. Notice Board (for service notices of diversions) 5. Bus Maps.
- [p347_c0004] ▪ Subway System Maps - System maps shall be installed in both the paid and unpaid zones of stations. In unpaid zones, they will be installed as part of the PIC. They shall be wall-mounted at typical platforms. At island platforms, maps will be installed in free-standing frame elements parallel to the track and located so that they do not obstruct the flow of passengers.
### [p347_c0005] Bus
[p347_c0006] Neither NYCT nor MTA Bus report on bus stops or bus shelters as these right-of-way amenity elements are maintained by the New York City Department of Transportation ("NYCDOT").

## Page 349

### [p349_c0001] Long Island Rail Road Metro-North Railroad
### [p349_c0002] MTA Long Island Rail Road and MTA Metro-North Railroad Major Service and Fare Change Analysis Policy, and System-wide Service Standards and Policies
[p349_c0003] In accordance with Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 (Title VI), MTA Long Island Rail Road (LIRR) and MTA Metro-North Railroad (MNR) have established their Major Service and Fare Change Policy and System-wide Service Standards and Policies.
### [p349_c0004] I. Long Island Rail Road and MTA Metro-North Railroad Major Service and Fare Change Analysis Policy
[p349_c0005] LIRR and MNR apply the following definitions and procedures in conducting their analyses of proposed major service changes and fare changes pursuant to Title VI.
### [p349_c0006] 1. Major Service Change
[p349_c0007] LIRR and MNR will conduct an analysis of those service changes that meet or exceed the definition of a "Major Service Change" provided below. Both railroads will use a statistical test to determine whether the proposed service change will have a disproportionate impact upon minority or low income riders with regard to, as applicable, train service, consist length, station, line segment, or service frequency.
[p349_c0008] LIRR and MNR define a Major Service Change as follows:
- [p349_c0009] • The addition or deletion of trains estimated to carry 25% or more of the total line ridership on the day of the week for which the change is made; • The addition or deletion of trains or cars constituting more than 25% of the revenue car miles operated on the line on the day of the week for which the change is proposed; • The establishment or discontinuance of service over a line segment; • The establishment or discontinuance of a station; or • A change in frequency of service to a station on a given day by more than 25%.
[p349_c0010] The following exceptions to the definition of Major Service Change apply:
- [p349_c0011] • Demonstration projects or experimental service; • Standard seasonal or holiday adjustments; • Temporary schedule changes to enable performance of line maintenance or capital improvement work; or • Temporary changes in response to emergency situations, service disruptions or events beyond the control of the railroads.
### [p349_c0012] 2. Fare Change
[p349_c0013] LIRR and MNR will conduct an analysis to determine whether a proposed fare change would result in a disparate impact on either minority or low income LIRR or MNR customers.
[p349_c0014] _(title vi railroad title vi policies (00024100))_
[p349_c0015] _Page 1 of 6_
[p349_c0016] _Master Page # 349 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 350

### [p350_c0001] Long Island Rail Road Metro-North Railroad
[p350_c0002] This analysis is based on data from the annual LIRR and MNR Customer Satisfaction Surveys and Origin/Destination Studies for each railroad, as well as other available socio-economic data (e.g., Census data).
### [p350_c0003] 3. Disparate Impact/Disproportionate Burden Policy
[p350_c0004] The following disparate impact and disproportionate burden policy applies to both LIRR and MNR. MNR and LIRR shall, prior to the implementation of any Major Service Change or fare change, evaluate whether the proposed change may result in adverse effects borne disproportionately by minority or low-income populations.
### [p350_c0005] Adverse Effects
[p350_c0006] An adverse effect exists where a “statistically significant” difference in service or cost causes a disparate impact or disproportionate burden to minority and/or low-income populations.
### [p350_c0007] Threshold
[p350_c0008] The threshold is determined by utilizing a statistical test based on available survey, census, operations, and other relevant information. The statistical tests used by the LIRR and MNR measure data to arrive at results with 95% degree of confidence and an error margin of plus or minus 5%. A “statistically significant” difference is demonstrated if the statistical result falls outside of the critical value. In such instances, MNR or LIRR will review the proposed change and consider measures to avoid, minimize or mitigate adverse effects.
### [p350_c0009] II. MTA Long Island Rail Road and MTA Metro-North Railroad System-wide Service Standards
[p350_c0010] The following system-wide service standards apply to LIRR and MNR operations.
### [p350_c0011] 1. Service Availability
[p350_c0012] Service Availability is a general measure of the distribution of routes within the areas serviced by LIRR and MNR.
[p350_c0013] The commuter rail routes are distributed to provide service to commuters who reside within the service territories of LIRR and MNR. These service territories are each defined as all census tracts that are within (and touching) 2.5 miles of all the commuter rail stations. For MNR, the service territory includes all MNR stations in Manhattan, the Bronx, Westchester, Putnam, Dutchess, Rockland and Orange Counties in New York State and Fairfield and New Haven Counties in Connecticut. For LIRR, the service territory includes all LIRR stations in Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens, Nassau and Suffolk Counties.
[p350_c0014] For purposes of conducting a Title VI analysis of service availability, the distance (distribution of stations – fixed access points) that a person must travel to gain entry to LIRR and/or MNR commuter rail services must be determined. The distance from the centroid of each census tract to the closest commuter rail station is computed and will be used for conducting such a Title VI analysis.
[p350_c0015] _(title vi railroad title vi policies (00024100))_
[p350_c0016] _Page 2 of 6_
[p350_c0017] _Master Page # 350 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 351

### [p351_c0001] Long Island Rail Road Metro-North Railroad
### [p351_c0002] 2. ● On Time Performance
[p351_c0003] On-time performance is a measure of runs completed as scheduled. At LIRR and MNR, a train is recorded as on time if it arrives at its final destination within five minutes and 59 seconds of its scheduled arrival. All trains operated should complete their assigned trips. Unless noted on the timetable, trains will not depart early from passenger stations where they are scheduled to receive passengers.
### [p351_c0004] 3. Vehicle Headway
[p351_c0005] Vehicle Headway is a measure of how often a train is scheduled to stop at a particular station. Maximum Vehicle Headway is based upon the station's level of service (determined by ridership by station or average ridership within specific operating line segments). Factors considered when determining service frequency also include availability of equipment, track scheduling, and operating resources.
[p351_c0006] Maximum Vehicle Headway differs for peak, reverse peak, weekday off-peak, and weekends.
### [p351_c0007] LIRR Maximum Headway Guideline
[p351_c0008] The chart below presents the Maximum Vehicle Headway by station level of service and time of day for LIRR stations in Zones 1-12. Zone 14 stations, Mets-Willets Point (only open seasonally for Mets home games and other special events), and Belmont (only open seasonally when races are taking place) are not covered by this guideline.
[p351_c0009]

```text
Level of Service Peak Rev. Peak Off-Peak Weekend Level 1* 20 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes Level 2 30 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes Level 3 45 minutes 90 minutes 90 minutes 90 minutes Level 4 60 minutes 120 minutes 120 minutes 120 minutes
```
[p351_c0010] _* Due to infrastructure constraints, Huntington and Ronkonkoma do not provide peak service at this headway. These constraints include: single track territory east of Farmingdale, existence of only two tracks west of Hicksville, and lack of a yard east of Huntington. Hunterspoint Avenue station does not provide service at Level 1 headways, because unlike the other LIRR stations, this station operates only weekday peak-period, peak-direction service._
[p351_c0011] _(title vi railroad title vi policies (00024100))_
[p351_c0012] _Master Page # 351 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 352

### [p352_c0001] Long Island Rail Road Metro-North Railroad
### [p352_c0002] MNR Maximum Headway Guideline
[p352_c0003] The chart below presents the maximum vehicle headway by operating line segment and time of day for MNR stations.
[p352_c0004]

```text
Line Segment Peak Rev. Peak Off-Peak Weekend Hudson Line Bronx 30 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes Mid-Hudson 25 minutes 30 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes Upper Hudson 30 minutes 30 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes Harlem Line Bronx 30 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes Mid-Harlem 20 minutes 30 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes Upper Harlem 25 minutes 30 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes Southeast - Wassaic 45 minutes 60 minutes 120 minutes 120 minutes New Haven Line Inner New Haven 20 minutes 30 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes Outer New Haven 25 minutes 30 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes New Canaan Branch 30 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes Danbury Branch 45 minutes 60 minutes 120 minutes 120 minutes Waterbury Branch 45 minutes 60 minutes 120 minutes 120 minutes Pascack Valley Line 45 minutes 60 minutes 120 minutes 120 minutes Port Jervis Line 30 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes 60 minutes
```
### [p352_c0005] 4. Loading Guidelines
[p352_c0006] Loading standards are used to determine seating capacity, to assign equipment (e.g., number/type of railcars), and to make subsequent adjustments by lengthening or shortening trains. Because a primary method of controlling costs is to minimize surplus car-miles while providing a seat for every customer, LIRR and MNR will adjust the assignment of cars and length of trains in accordance with the below occupancy policies.
[p352_c0007] Train lengths are adjusted to conform to the loading standard using train-by-train ridership data, which is monitored throughout the year. Train lengths are modified to ensure that adequate seating is provided while controlling the total car-miles operated.
### [p352_c0008] Maximum Recommended Occupancy Policy:
[p352_c0009]

```text
Lengthening Trains If Occupancy Exceeds... Shortening Trains If Occupancy (after reduction) Would Not Exceed... AM Peak, PM Peak, Reverse Peak 95% 95% Off-Peak Weekday 85% 85% Weekend 75% 75%
```
[p352_c0010] _(title vi railroad title vi policies (00024100))_
[p352_c0011] _Page 4 of 6_
[p352_c0012] _Master Page # 352 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 353

### [p353_c0001] Long Island Rail Road Metro-North Railroad
[p353_c0002] Holiday/Special Event Adjustments: Adjustments to the regular equipment assignments are made to account for changes in travel patterns and demand on holidays and holiday weekends and other special events throughout the year.
### [p353_c0003] III. MTA Long Island Rail Road and MTA Metro-North Railroad System-wide Service Policies
[p353_c0004] The following system-wide service policies apply to both LIRR and MNR.
### [p353_c0005] 1. Vehicle Assignment Policy
[p353_c0006] Both LIRR and MNR operate diesel and electric powered trains.
[p353_c0007] The primary criterion in assigning transit vehicles is the type of propulsion power required for a particular branch or line segment. Diesel locomotive-hauled coaches are used on non-electrified territory (Main Line east of Ronkonkoma, Oyster Bay, Port Jefferson, and Montauk branches on the LIRR; Hudson Line north of Croton-Harmon, Harlem Line north of Southeast, Danbury Branch and Waterbury Branch on MNR). Electric Multiple-Unit (EMU) vehicles are used on electrified territory (all remaining lines/branches). Where a train operates over both electrified and non-electrified territory, diesel locomotive hauled coaches must be assigned.
[p353_c0008] All coaches and EMUs have similar amenities including air conditioning, rest rooms, and like decor which provide the same level of customer comfort and convenience. Vehicles are assigned as required, with seating capacity and maintenance cycles driving the assignments. Cars are not assigned to specific routes or branches within electric or diesel territory but are cycled from line/branch to line/branch to achieve optimum car utilization efficiency. Short-term rolling stock assignment plans are developed for deployment of railcars.
[p353_c0009] Average Age of Fleet: Vehicles are assigned to trains based on required propulsion power (diesel or electric) for the route, individual train ridership and seating capacity, and maintenance and storage yard requirements.
### [p353_c0010] 2. Transit Amenities
[p353_c0011] Transit amenities are items of comfort and convenience made available to railroad customers.
[p353_c0012] Amenities available at train stations can include benches, waiting rooms, platform shelters, restrooms, vending machines, information kiosks, recycling/trash bins, public address (PA) speakers, visual information displays, escalators, elevators, and ramps. The station amenities provided are based on a station's daily ridership, length of platform, and size of station, but may be limited or constrained by physical layout, available space, and utility infrastructure constraints (e.g., local commercial electric power availability). Stations are categorized into levels; stations in the highest ridership category receive the full range of amenities if available space allows.
[p353_c0013] _(title vi railroad title vi policies (00024100))_

### [p354_c0001] Long Island Rail Road Metro-North Railroad
[p354_c0002] Amenities onboard trains include heating and air conditioning, interior lighting, bathrooms, baggage racks, and public address systems. All trains regardless of car type (coaches or multiple-units) and method of propulsion (diesel or electric) are equipped with similar amenities.

## Page 357

### [p357_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p357_c0002] Long Island Rail Road Transit Monitoring
[p357_c0003] The FTA requires that transit providers, such as the MTA and its affiliated agencies, monitor, not less than every three (3) years, the performance of their transit system relative to their system-wide service standards and service policies. The Long Island Rail Road annual monitoring report is prepared and submitted to the MTA Department of Diversity and Civil Rights as part of its monitoring and oversight of MTA's operating agencies Title VI compliance. The Data contained herein becomes part of the MTA-wide consolidated Title VI Program reviewed and approved by all internal stakeholders, including the MTA Board of Directors and submitted to the Federal Transit Administration (FTA) on a triennial basis. (Exhibit 1).
[p357_c0004] MTA's Board approved standards and policies are presented in detail in a previous section of this Report entitled "Requirement to Set System-wide Service Standards and Policies" and include standards and policies for such factors as: Vehicle Load, Vehicle Headway, On-Time Performance, Service Availability, Transit Amenities and Vehicle Assignment.
[p357_c0005] Each of these six (6) factors, are monitored statistically, and separately for all modes, using a combination of quantitative techniques: the statistical t-test, the chi-square statistical test and ridership surveys which help to define the actual experiences of individual passengers. The MTA uses these quantitative measures of testing to ascertain if there are significant differences between either minority/non-minority and/or income groups being measured for service equity analyses. Use of either of the two statistical tests is dependent on the type of data being analyzed.
### [p357_c0006] Assessments:
[p357_c0007] As a summary of the Long Island Rail Road July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025, Title VI associated quantitative analyses of the current service procedures (standards) the following assessment of compliance, predicated on the minority based comparisons is presented. For the sake of brevity, we have presented the findings in the tables below.
[p357_c0008] Detailed analytical data, showing the numerical calculations is available upon request. Please note, in the case of an identified inequity, then Long Island Rail Road will show that inequity with either its proposed corrective action or the reason why corrective action is not appropriate, that is, the inequity is neither a

```text
Service Standards and Policies Service Measure Standard Analysis Results – Minority Analysis Results – Poverty Analysis Notes/Mitigation Service Availability The LIRR Service Area consists of all Census tracts within (and touching) a 2.5-mile radius from all Stations. Per thresholds developed for the NYC and Long Island service areas, minority/non-minority and above/at-or-below poverty designations are assigned to each tract. A statistical analysis is performed to determine if there is any difference between the station distance for randomly selected minority/non-minority tracts and above/at-or-below poverty tracts. t-test No significant difference No significant difference Vehicle Load* LIRR Service Standards include guideline load factors of .95 for the AM Peak and .85 for Off-Peak A statistical analysis is performed to determine if there is any difference between load factors on minority/non-minority and above/at-or-below poverty branches. t-test No significant difference No significant difference Vehicle Headway* LIRR Service Standards include maximum headway guidelines by station level of service and time of day. A statistical analysis is performed to determine if there is any difference between average headways on minority/non-minority and above/at-or-below poverty branches. t-test No significant difference Statistically significant difference during off-peak (Favorable to Low Income) Lower headways on low-income branches at all times. On-time Performance* Measure of trains arriving at their final destination within 5:59 of their scheduled arrival time. A statistical analysis is performed to determine if there is any difference between the On-time Performance for minority/non-minority and above/at-or-below poverty branches. t-test No significant difference No significant difference Vehicle Assignment* LIRR assigns its passenger fleet to branches dependent on the propulsion type on that branch. Both electric and diesel cars are assigned as required without regard to car age. A statistical analysis is performed to determine if there is any difference between the fleet age for cars that serve minority/non-minority and above/at-or-below poverty branches. t-test No significant difference No significant difference
```
[p359_c0004] *Vehicle Load, Vehicle Headway, On-time Performance, and Vehicle Assignment are all service measures that are monitored at the branch level. Pursuant to the FTA Title VI circular, a branch is designated as a Minority Branch if at least one-third of its total route mileage falls in one or more minority Census tracts. A branch is designated as an At-or-Below Poverty Branch if at least one-third of its total route mileage falls into one or more at-or-below poverty Census tracts.

## Page 361

### [p361_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p361_c0002] Introduction
[p361_c0003] The Long Island Rail Road monitors the performance of our transit system relative to the Board-approved system-wide service standards and service policies and reports annually on the results of its monitoring efforts to the MTA's Department of Diversity and Civil Rights (DDCR). A triennial report is also prepared for inclusion in the MTA-wide submission to the FTA.
[p361_c0004] The Long Island Rail Road uses quantitative measures of testing: the t-test and Chi-square statistical tests, in order to ascertain if there are significant differences between either minority /non-minority and/or income groups being measured for service equity analyses. Use of either of these two statistical tests is dependent on the type of data being analyzed. The service equity monitoring showed that the application of the Standards and Policies resulted in no disparate impact or disproportionate burden on minority or low-income communities.
[p361_c0005] Long Island Rail Road monitoring assessments, which are used to determine whether disparate impacts exist on the basis of race, color, or national origin, were completed for each of the board- approved standards and policies, for the minority and non-minority populations. These assessments are detailed, along with the monitoring results, in Parts II and III of this Exhibit.
### [p361_c0006] Test Method
[p361_c0007] LIRR uses the following techniques to monitor the performance of the transit system relative to system-wide service standards and policies:
- [p361_c0008] * Service Availability: A t-test statistical analysis is used to see if there was any difference in the distances to the closest railroad stations, between the randomly selected minority & non-minority populations and for the randomly selected above poverty and at-or-below poverty populations. * Vehicle Load: A t-test statistical analysis is performed to see if there was any significant difference between the services provided for the minority branches and the non-minority branches and for the above poverty and at-or-below poverty branches. * Vehicle Headway: A t-test statistical analysis is performed to see if there was any significant difference between the level of service provided for the minority routes and the non-minority routes and for the above poverty and at-or-below poverty routes. * On-Time Performance: A t-test statistical analysis is performed to see if there was any significant difference between the on-time performance of vehicles on the minority routes and the non-minority routes and for the above poverty and at-or-below poverty routes. * Vehicle Assignment: A t-test statistical analysis is performed to see if there was any significant difference between the average age of the equipment used on the minority branch and the non-minority branches and for the above poverty and at-or-below poverty routes. * Transit Amenities: Chi-Square analysis is performed on station amenities with established standards and guidelines to determine if there is any difference in amenity distribution between the stations used in the minority and non-minority Census tracts and those used in the above poverty and at-or-below poverty tracts.
[p361_c0009] _Page 6_
[p361_c0010] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p361_c0011] _Master Page # 361 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 367

### [p367_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p367_c0002] A.1. Minority-Based Analysis – Service Availability
### [p367_c0003] Data
[p367_c0004] Service Availability is a general measure of the distribution of routes within the MTA Long Island Rail Road (LIRR) service area.
[p367_c0005] In order to compare the distance traveled to gain access to LIRR service between the minority and non-minority areas, the New York City and Long Island census tracts were separated into minority and non-minority tracts.
[p367_c0006] LIRR service area includes all census tracts that are within (and touching) 2.5 miles of all LIRR railroad stations in Manhattan, Queens, Brooklyn, Nassau and Suffolk counties.
[p367_c0007] For the LIRR New York City service area (of the 5 counties making up New York City, the LIRR serves 3: Queens, Kings (Brooklyn) and New York (Manhattan)), a census tract is considered minority if the minority population of the tracts selected in the service area is equal to or greater than the service area's minority population of 67.66%. For the Long Island counties of Nassau and Suffolk service area (the LIRR serves both), a census tract is considered minority if the minority population of that tract is equal to, or greater than the Long Island service area's minority population of 39.65%. Statistics were taken from the Census Bureau's 5 year American Communities Survey (2018-2022).
[p367_c0008] In order to compare the distance traveled to gain access to LIRR service between minority and non-minority areas, the New York City and Long Island census tracts were separated into minority and non-minority tracts.
[p367_c0009] 200 of all the LIRR service area census tracts were randomly selected for analysis (100 from the minority tracts and 100 from the non-minority tracts). The distance from the centroid of each census tract to the closest LIRR station was measured. Additionally, our sampling methodology was done by region, (20% NYC and 80% LI service areas) reflecting LIRR ridership patterns.
[p367_c0010] Table 1 lists each randomly selected tract number (grouped by minority status), the distance to the closest LIRR station from the centroid of the tract, and the closest LIRR station
[p367_c0011] _Page 12_

## Page 378

### [p378_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p378_c0002] A.1. Minority-Based Analysis – Service Availability (Continued)
### [p378_c0003] Analysis and Results
[p378_c0004] To determine if the LIRR service provided is equitable with respect to minority and non-minority census tracts, a comparison of service availability was performed. A t -test statistical analysis was used to see if there was any difference in the distances to the closest railroad stations, between the randomly selected minority & non-minority populations.
[p378_c0005] Based on the results below, we can conclude that there appears to be no inequity between the minority and the non-minority populations as it relates to service availability in the LIRR service areas.
[p378_c0006]

```text
MINORITY NON-MINORITY Mean Distance (miles) 1.31 1.46 Standard Deviation (miles) 0.73 0.75
```
[p378_c0007] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (for a 95% confidence), the resulting t -statistic = -1.38 . The t -critical values are +/- 1.97. Since -1.38 > -1.97 and < +1.97, we can therefore conclude that there is no statistical difference between the distance minority populations and non-minority populations in the LIRR service area are to LIRR stations.
[p378_c0008] _Page 23_
[p378_c0009] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p378_c0010] _Master Page # 378 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 379

### [p379_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p379_c0002] A.2. Minority-Based Analysis – Vehicle Load Factor
### [p379_c0003] Data
[p379_c0004] The Long Island Rail Road load factor standards are designed to be responsive to passenger demands. The overall load factor standard is one (1) train car for every 115* boarding passengers on M3 electric cars, one (1) train car for every 106* boarding passengers on M7 and M9 electric cars. As of June 30, 2025, 9% of the available MU (multiple unit) fleet was M3 cars, 73% was M7 cars and 18% was M9 cars. Therefore, the average number of seats per car was calculated as 107.5.
[p379_c0005] In addition, the overall load factor standard is one (1) train car for every 140 boarding passengers on diesel bi-level cars per station east of Jamaica. A review of train size is initiated when train loading exceeds or falls below established load point factors. The LIRR goal is to provide adequate seating during the morning and evening peak period. The Long Island Rail Road monitors and reviews vehicle load data on an on-going basis and, wherever feasible, reallocates human and equipment resources to meet passenger demand.
[p379_c0006] Table 1 is the load factor analysis based on the 2024 Ridership Count 1 . This table illustrates that the LIRR has been responsive to passenger seating demands. The table shows the peak one (1) hour period load factor for all branches on the LIRR. Peak hour trains are defined as those trains arriving at the western terminals, or providing connecting service to trains arriving at western terminals, between 8:00 a.m. and 9:00 a.m.
[p379_c0007] Table 2 shows the off-peak period load factor for all branches on the LIRR. Off-Peak is defined as those trains arriving at the western terminals, or providing connecting service to trains arriving at western terminals, between 10:00 a.m. and 4:00 p.m.
[p379_c0008] Note: A branch is denoted as a Minority Branch if at least one third of its total route mileage falls in one or more minority census tracts. Any station that is located within a minority census tract is considered a minority station.
[p379_c0009] *Number of seats on M3, M7 and M9 cars is an average. M7 and M9 cars seating varies, one car in each pair is equipped with a handicapped accessible restroom and therefore has less seats than its mate. In addition, minor modifications have been undertaken to increase seating on some M7 cars.
[p379_c0010] _1 Data that was available as of 6/30/2024_
[p379_c0011] _Page 24_
[p379_c0012] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p379_c0013] _Master Page # 379 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 382

### [p382_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p382_c0002] A.2. Minority-Based Analysis – Vehicle Load Factor (Continued)
### [p382_c0003] Analysis and Results
[p382_c0004] A t-test statistical analysis was performed to see if there was any significant difference between the services provided for the minority branches and the non-minority branches. The results for the am peak and off peak time periods are listed below.
### [p382_c0005] AM PEAK
[p382_c0006]

```text
MINORITY NON-MINORITY Guideline Average Load Factor 0.95 0.95 Actual Average Load Factor 0.59 0.69 Average Difference 0.36 0.26 Variance 0.023 0.004
```
[p382_c0007] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (95% confidence), the resulting t-statistic = 1.50 . The t-critical values are +/-2.36. Since 1.50 > -2.36 and < +2.36, we can therefore conclude that there is no significant difference in the AM peak train service provided between the minority and non-minority branches.
### [p382_c0008] OFF PEAK
[p382_c0009]

```text
MINORITY NON-MINORITY Guideline Average Load Factor 0.85 0.85 Actual Average Load Factor 0.23 0.22 Average Difference 0.62 0.63 Variance 0.00 0.00
```
[p382_c0010] Using a two-tailed test of hypothesis with a 5% error (95% confidence), the resulting t-statistic = -0.14 . The t-critical values are +/-2.26. Since -0.14 > -2.26 and < +2.26, we can therefore conclude that there is no significant difference in the off peak train service provided between the minority and non-minority branches.
[p382_c0011] _Page 27_
[p382_c0012] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p382_c0013] _Master Page # 382 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 383

### [p383_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p383_c0002] A.3. Minority-Based Analysis – Vehicle Headway
### [p383_c0003] Data
[p383_c0004] In determining train headways, many factors are considered. The first is ridership demand. Service is scheduled to ensure that, where possible, all customers can expect a seated ride to their destination. Minimum levels of service to ensure customer convenience and comfort are based on Long Island Rail Road Service Guidelines. Annual ridership checks are performed on all scheduled trains to determine if LIRR service meets current ridership demand and Service Guideline standards.
[p383_c0005] In addition to ridership demand, vehicle headways are significantly influenced by crew and equipment availability. After determining the proper number of trains per hour based on ridership demand, the equipment to be operated and the crews to operate the equipment must be manipulated throughout the LIRR system to assure that all train intervals can be covered with maximum efficiency.
[p383_c0006] The number of available train slots within our system influences the number of trains that can be scheduled. Vehicle headways and capacity can be influenced by construction projects that affect infrastructure availability. The available infrastructure in a given area determines the maximum number of trains that can operate over a given portion of railroad. In some instances available time slots can also be impacted by service in the opposing direction. Vehicles headways are scheduled to meet Service Guidelines while assuring that the number and frequency of trains does not exceed the track and/or signal capacity in that given area.
[p383_c0007] The passenger train schedules provide the most current information as it pertains to the LIRR's vehicle headways. The schedules indicate the scheduled frequency of service provided by the LIRR. The LIRR considers the morning peak to be from 6:00 am until 10:00 am and the evening peak to be from 4:00 pm to 8:00 pm during the weekday.
[p383_c0008] The following table lists the actual average branch headways as of June 30, 2025.
[p383_c0009] _Page 28_
[p383_c0010] _LIRR Title VI Program Update: July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025_
[p383_c0011] _Master Page # 383 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 384

### [p384_c0001] Exhibit 11_Service Monitoring 2026_LIRR
### [p384_c0002] VEHICLE HEADWAY (minutes)
[p384_c0003]

```text
BRANCH AM PEAK PM PEAK OFF PEAK BABYLON 9 11 19 * FAR ROCKAWAY 17 28 53 * HEMPSTEAD 25 25 58 * HUNTINGTON 15 18 30 * LONG BEACH 18 24 55 OYSTER BAY 39 50 75 PATCHOGUE/SPEONK/MONTAUK 26 22 57 PORT JEFFERSON 27 33 71 * PORT WASHINGTON 16 13 29 RONKONKOMA ^^ 12 14 29 * WEST HEMPSTEAD 52 40 59
```
[p384_c0004] All Branches have minority stations

[p384_c0006] Notes: Train stopping patterns vary to equalize passenger loadings and minimize standees.
[p384_c0007] There are no headway minutes for Greenport during the AM and PM Peak because there is no continuous train service during the peak periods.
