## Page 1613

### [p1613_c0001] I. INTRODUCTION
[p1613_c0002] This Title VI Plan submission was prepared in accordance with the current FTA guidance set forth in FTA Circular 4702.1B, "Title VI Requirements and Guidelines for Federal Transit Administration Recipients," dated October 1, 2012.
[p1613_c0003] The Metropolitan Transportation Authority ("MTA") and its affiliated and subsidiary agencies: MTA Long Island Rail Road ("LIRR"), MTA Metro-North Railroad ("MNR"), MTA Bus Company ("MTA Bus"), MTA Construction and Development Company ("C&D"), MTA Grand Central Madison Operating Company ("GCMOC"), and MTA New York City Transit ("NYCT"), including the Manhattan and Bronx Surface Transit Operating Authority ("MaBSTOA") and the Staten Island Rapid Transit Operating Authority ("SIRTOA"), (collectively, "the MTA" or "MTA Agencies") 1 are committed to the tenets of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, and the assurances of equal opportunity and access to the delivery of transit services, benefits and amenities to customers throughout its service area.
[p1613_c0004] The Long Island Rail Road ("LIRR") system is comprised of over 700 miles of track on 11 different branches, stretching 120 miles from Montauk—on the eastern tip of Long Island—to Manhattan's Penn Station and Grand Central Madison Terminal, as well as Atlantic Terminal in Brooklyn. Along the way, the LIRR serves 126 stations in Nassau, Suffolk, Queens, Kings, and New York (Manhattan) counties, providing service for over 78.6 million customers each year. As of June 30, 2025, LIRR carried approximately 264,000 customers each weekday on 948 daily trains.
[p1613_c0005] Metro-North Railroad ("MNR") is responsible for operating commuter rail service from the northern and western suburbs of New York City and the western suburbs of Connecticut to the New York City central business district. With approximately 385 route miles and 800 miles of track, MNR serves 86 stations in the following New York State counties: Dutchess, Putnam, Westchester, Bronx, New York, Rockland, and Orange. The railroad also serves 38 stations in the Greater Bridgeport, Naugatuck Valley, South Central and Western planning regions in the state of Connecticut. 2 As of June 30, 2025, Metro-North carried approximately 240,151 customers each weekday. East of Hudson ridership was 235,450; West of Hudson ridership was 4,701.
[p1613_c0006] The MTA Bus Company ("MTA Bus") was created in September 2004 to commence local and express bus services in areas formerly provided by seven (7) private bus companies that operated under franchises granted by the New York City Department of Transportation (NYCDOT). MTA Bus Company operates 46 local routes and 3 Select Bus Service routes serving the counties of Bronx, Kings, Nassau, New York and Queens and 43 express bus routes serving the counties of Bronx, Kings, Nassau, New York, Queens and Westchester. The MTA bus fleet, which consists of New York City Transit and MTA Bus, has
[p1613_c0007] _1 The MTA Triborough Bridge and Tunnel Authority, an MTA affiliate, is not subject to this Policy Directive due to its funding status._
[p1613_c0008] _2 In July 2022, the Census Bureau officially replaced New Haven County and Fairfield County with planning regions._

[p1613_c0011] _Master Page # 1613 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 1614

[p1614_c0001] a combined fleet of 5,915 vehicles. As of June 30, 2025, MTA Bus Company served approximately 294,337 average daily weekday riders.
[p1614_c0002] New York City Transit (“NYCT”) is the largest public transportation agency in North America and one of the largest in the world. The ridership data as of June 30, 2025, reflects average weekday subway ridership of approximately 4.4 million customers, and bus ridership of 2.3 million. NYCT has a fleet of more than 6,762 subway cars that traveled approximately 374 million miles along 655 miles of track, 24 hours a day, seven days a week. There are 472 subway stations, including 150 currently accessible via elevators and ramps to customers who have mobility impairments. NYCT’s subway system serves Kings, Bronx, Manhattan, Richmond (Staten Island Railway), and Queens counties, making an average of 6.9 million weekday trips. NYCT’s bus fleet includes 192 local routes, 33 express routes, and 17 Select Bus Service routes throughout the five boroughs.
[p1614_c0003] The MTA Department of Diversity and Civil Rights (DDCR) has developed this consolidated 3-year Title VI Plan for submission to the FTA in accordance with Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 (42 U.S.C. § 2000d et seq.); the Federal Transit Laws (49 U.S.C. § 5332); and the Federal Transit Administration (FTA) Title VI guidance and requirements in effect during the plan period, as set forth in Circular 4702.1B; and FTA Environmental Justice guidance and requirements in effect during the plan period as set forth in Circular 4703.1. Note that although FTA withdrew Circular 4703.1 (Environmental Justice Policy Guidance for Federal Transit Administration Recipients) on September 15, 2025, the Circular and associated requirements were in place during entirety of the program period covered in this submission.
[p1614_c0004] Title VI prohibits discrimination in federally assisted programs and provides that no person shall, on the grounds of race, color, or national origin (including Limited English Proficiency (LEP)), be excluded from participation in, be denied the benefits of, or be otherwise subjected to discrimination under any program or activity receiving federal assistance. Related federal nondiscrimination laws further prohibit discrimination on the basis of religion, age, sex, and disability. These additional protected categories are considered and protected within Title VI, consistent with FTA interpretation and administration as set forth in Circular 4702.1B.
[p1614_c0005] Janno Lieber, Chair and Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of the MTA, is ultimately responsible for the overall implementation, administration, and monitoring of the MTA Title VI Plan. In turn, the Chair/CEO has delegated this responsibility to the Agency’s Chief Diversity and Inclusion Officer (CDIO) of the MTA Department of Diversity and Civil Rights (DDCR). The CDIO reports directly to the Chair/CEO.
[p1614_c0006] The CDIO develops, directs, and manages the Agency’s strategic, long- and short-range Title VI plans and endeavors to ensure that they align with the overarching efforts of the organization. More specifically, the CDIO provides strategic oversight for the MTA Title VI Plan, provides assurances to the FTA of compliance, and communicates compliance status information to the MTA Chair/CEO, Board of Directors, and Executive Management Team. Also, the MTA Agencies’ Chief Equal Opportunity Officers (EO Officers) are responsible for implementing and monitoring Title VI policies at their designated agencies. These efforts are accomplished through the continuous review and monitoring of policies, procedures, practices, compliance audit findings, service and fare equity analyses, and planning processes.
[p1614_c0007] _New York Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA) - Title VI Plan Submission 2026_
[p1614_c0008] _Page 2 of 16_
[p1614_c0009] _Master Page # 1614 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 1615

[p1615_c0001] The data reported in this Title VI Plan is reflective of the period of July 1, 2022, through June 30, 2025, and has been reviewed and approved by the MTA Board of Directors as documented in Exhibit 1 .
### [p1615_c0002] II. TITLE VI NOTICE TO THE PUBLIC
[p1615_c0003] The MTA's current Title VI Notice to the Public includes the following statement: "The Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA) and its affiliated and subsidiary agencies are committed to ensuring that no one is excluded from participation in, denied the benefits of, or otherwise subjected to, discrimination in our programs, services, or activities on the basis of race, color, or national origin (including limited English proficiency) as protected by Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, and age, sex, disability, or religion as protected by other Federal Transit Laws, (49 U.S.C. § 5332)." Dissemination of the MTA's Title VI Notice to the Public includes posting on the MTA website and within the lobbies of MTA office buildings that are accessible to customers as well as various subway, commuter rail stations, and terminals.
[p1615_c0004] The Notice to the Public and the Title VI Complaint Form and Procedures described in the next section are maintained on the MTA website and can be converted into multiple languages using Google Translate. The materials have also been officially translated into the top nine predominantly spoken non-English languages of the MTA's entire service area which includes Spanish, Chinese, Russian, Yiddish, Bengali, Haitian Creole, Korean, Italian, and Arabic. Samples of the MTA Title VI Notice to the Public are attached in Exhibit 2 .
### [p1615_c0005] III. TITLE VI COMPLAINT PROCEDURE
[p1615_c0006] The MTA's Board has approved an all-agency Title VI and Related Nondiscrimination Laws Complaint Management policy which governs the process used to investigate and track all complaints that allege discrimination on the grounds of race, color, national origin (including Limited English Proficiency (LEP)), age, sex, religion and disability. The policy directive and the accompanying Complaint Management Forms have been universally adopted by all MTA operating agencies for managing and resolving complaints alleging discrimination in the provision of MTA services and activities provided to the public. A copy is attached as Exhibit 3 .
[p1615_c0007] Information on how to submit a Title VI complaint is provided to the public via the MTA website . A fillable complaint form is also available on the site along with instructions for the customer to print the form and submit it to the appropriate MTA Agency Chief EO Officer. These documents have been translated into the top nine predominantly non-English spoken languages of the MTA's entire service area, as listed above in Section II. Additional translations are available on the MTA website through Google Translate and by contacting the MTA at 511.
[p1615_c0008] The MTA operating agencies monitor, track, and report all Title VI complaints received within their respective agencies. Specifically, complaints are logged and maintained in an electronic database, classified using the alleged discrimination basis, and reported quarterly to the Diversity Committee of the MTA Board of Directors.
[p1615_c0009] _New York Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA) - Title VI Plan Submission 2026_
[p1615_c0010] _Page 3 of 16_
[p1615_c0011] _Master Page # 1615 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 1616

[p1616_c0001] MTA Agencies acknowledge receipt of all complaints and make every attempt to complete investigations of complaints within 120 calendar days of receipt. Upon completion, a written determination explaining the outcome of the investigation is sent to both the Complainant and the Respondent. When a complaint is filed against an employee, the Respondent's supervisor will be notified, and if a cause finding is issued, the supervisor will receive instructions regarding the implementation of appropriate personnel action and next steps.
### [p1616_c0002] IV. TRANSIT-RELATED TITLE VI INVESTIGATIONS, COMPLAINTS AND LAWSUITS
[p1616_c0003] During the report period of July 1, 2022 – June 30, 2025, the MTA agencies received 258 Title VI complaints and 3 Title VI lawsuits. Currently there are 0 pending Title VI investigations being conducted by FTA against any of the MTA Agencies. A table summarizing investigations, complaints, and lawsuits during this reporting term is attached in Exhibit 4 .
### [p1616_c0004] V. PUBLIC PARTICIPATION PLAN
[p1616_c0005] The MTA supports, values and promotes the comprehensive participation of all members of the public, including, but not limited to, minorities, people with disabilities, low-income populations, and people who speak English less than very well. The goal is to ensure that participation is accessible and contributes to a more robust and representative decision-making process that strengthens the New York metropolitan area economy.
[p1616_c0006] The MTA's Government and Community Relations (GCR) Department significantly contributes to the effectiveness of the MTA's public outreach and MTA's Title VI Plan by utilizing a Public Participation Guide ("Guide") as a key resource. The Guide describes the overall goals, guiding principles, and outreach methods that the MTA uses to reach its riders and affected communities. The Guide is attached as Exhibit 5 .
[p1616_c0007] More specifically, the Guide seeks to ensure that:
- [p1616_c0008] • Potentially affected community members will be afforded a meaningful opportunity to participate in decisions regarding proposed activities, and their involvement is actively sought and facilitated; and • Public input plays an important role in the MTA's overall decision-making process.
[p1616_c0009] The Guide serves as a framework for public participation when:
- [p1616_c0010] • A fare change (increase or decrease) is being considered; • A change in fare media is being considered; • A major service change, as defined by the MTA's service standards, is being considered; • Eminent domain proceedings are anticipated; • Environmental Impact Statements (EIS) are required;
[p1616_c0011] _New York Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA) - Title VI Plan Submission 2026_
[p1616_c0012] _Page 4 of 16_
[p1616_c0013] _Master Page # 1616 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 1617

- [p1617_c0001] • FTA funding requests are being submitted; • Capital Programs are under development; and/or • Other MTA programs or projects require public participation, education or awareness.
[p1617_c0002] From July 1, 2022, to June 30, 2025, MTA Agencies conducted or participated in 16 public hearings and over 900 community outreach and other public meetings during various times and at multiple transit and ADA-accessible locations. These events were organized to provide customers with complete information, timely public notice, and full access to opportunities for early and continuing involvement in key decisions related to major service changes, fare increases and the transportation planning process.
[p1617_c0003] A listing of public participation events that the MTA conducted or attended during the plan period is attached in Exhibit 6 .
### [p1617_c0004] VI. LANGUAGE ASSISTANCE PLAN (LAP)
[p1617_c0005] The MTA reaffirms its commitment to providing interpreter support and written translation services at every point of contact, both in person and over the phone, for individuals with limited English proficiency or speech and hearing impairments.
[p1617_c0006] The LAP assists individuals with limited English proficiency to understand and ensure equal benefit of MTA services, programs, and safety information.
[p1617_c0007] Each of the MTA's operating agencies 3 developed a LAP aligned with the MTA's overall mission to provide a transit system that is safe, reliable, and accessible for all, ensuring millions of riders get where they need to go. Together, the MTA's bridges, commuter rails, subways and buses form a resilient and accessible network that keeps communities connected and functioning, especially during emergencies.
[p1617_c0008] Each MTA Agency conducted a four-factor analysis, using the United States Census Bureau's American Community Survey ("ACS") data, along with other data sources identified in their Plans, to determine 1) the Limited English Proficiency (LEP) populations within the MTA's service areas; 2) the frequency with which LEP individuals come into contact with the MTA's programs, activities and services; 3) the nature and importance of the programs and services; and 4) the resources available to the MTA for LEP outreach.
[p1617_c0009] Based on these assessments, for the period of July 1, 2022, through June 30, 2025, MTA Agencies were successful in providing a significant number of oral and written language assistance services to LEP populations. In addition, guidelines have been developed for identifying and translating vital documents and local service information based upon the demographic make-up of the impacted service area and requests from community and public advocacy groups.
[p1617_c0010] _3 LIRR, MNR, MTA Bus Company, and NYCT_

## Page 1618

[p1618_c0001] The MTA has developed an all Agency-wide LAP. The MTA LAP ties together the individual agency plans, providing a consolidated overview that outlines the policies and procedures successfully implemented across the MTA to ensure meaningful access to all services for all individuals. The MTA LAP is provided in Exhibit 7 , and MTA data sources and examples are provided in Exhibit 7a .
### [p1618_c0002] VII. MINORITY REPRESENTATION ON PLANNING AND ADVISORY BODIES
[p1618_c0003] The MTA is governed by a 23-member Board of Directors (the MTA Board). Members are nominated by the Governor of New York, with four recommended by New York City's Mayor and one each by the County Executives of Nassau, Suffolk, Westchester, Dutchess, Orange, Rockland, and Putnam counties. The members representing the latter four cast one collective vote. All Board members are confirmed by the New York State Senate. The Board also has six rotating, non-voting seats held by representatives of organized labor and the Permanent Citizens Advisory Committee (PCAC), which serves as a voice for users of MTA transit and commuter facilities.
[p1618_c0004] PCAC consists of 38 members who regularly commute on Long Island Rail Road, Metro-North Railroad, New York City Transit, and/or MTA Bus Company. Its mission is to advise the MTA on behalf of subway, bus, and commuter rail users in the formulation and implementation of MTA policy and service, and to hold the MTA Board and MTA management accountable to riders.
[p1618_c0005] Non-Elected Committees – During this reporting period, the MTA did not have any transit-related, non-elected planning boards, advisory councils or committees, of which the membership was selected by the MTA.
### [p1618_c0006] VIII. SUB-RECIPIENTS ASSISTANCE AND MONITORING
[p1618_c0007] During this reporting period, the MTA did not have any sub-recipients.
### [p1618_c0008] IX. DETERMINATION OF SITE OR LOCATION OF FACILITIES
[p1618_c0009] The MTA did not determine any new sites or locations for facilities for storage or maintenance facilities, operations centers, etc., during the report period. The MTA has no new facilities planned within the next three-year period as shown in Exhibit 8 .
### [p1618_c0010] X. EQUITY ANALYSIS – LOCATIONS, FACILITIES AND NEW STARTS PROJECTS
[p1618_c0011] The MTA did not apply for New Starts or Small Starts funding during the period July 1, 2022 , through June 30, 2025 .
[p1618_c0012] The MTA had no federally funded facilities construction projects such as vehicle storage or maintenance facilities, or any New Starts projects, that required an equity analysis during this program period.

### [p1619_c0001] MTA 2020-2024 and 2025-2029 CAPITAL PROGRAMS:
[p1619_c0002] In January 2020, the MTA Board approved its 2020–2024 Capital Program totaling $54.8 billion. The Capital Plan was designed to build on past achievements, ensuring that improvements in customer satisfaction, service reliability and safety will be sustainable for years to come. A list of Capital Projects that covered July 1, 2022 – December 31, 2024, is provided in Exhibit 9a . Assuming a fully funded 2020–2024 Capital Plan, the 2025–2029 Plan looks towards the next slate of vital improvement projects, informed by a system-wide evaluation the MTA’s Twenty-Year Needs Assessment. See Exhibit 9b .
### [p1619_c0003] Environmental Justice Analyses
[p1619_c0004] No Environmental Justice (EJ) analyses were required during the plan period.
### [p1619_c0005] XI. SERVICE STANDARDS AND SERVICE POLICIES
[p1619_c0006] In accordance with the requirements of FTA Circular 4702.1B, Chapter IV, the MTA implemented Service Standards and Policies for Subway, Bus, and Commuter Rail modes of service.
[p1619_c0007] The guiding document for the standards and policies for NYCT (Subway and Bus) and MTA Bus was approved by the MTA Board on June 3, 2013, and is provided in Exhibit 10 . The guiding document for the standards and policies for LIRR and MNR was also approved by the MTA Board on June 3, 2013, and is also provided in Exhibit 10 .
[p1619_c0008] MTA is currently conducting a review of the Service Standards and Service Policies. As part of this process, the agencies will determine whether updates are warranted to ensure continued Title VI compliance and alignment with operational needs. If the review determines updates are necessary, the MTA will identify and pursue next steps to draft and implement updates and, as applicable, incorporate finalized changes in updated versions during the next Title VI Plan update period. The sections below highlight a few Service Policy activities that took place during the reporting period.
### [p1619_c0009] Service Policy Activity Highlights
### [p1619_c0010] NYCT Subway and Bus and MTA Bus Company

OMNY machines are designed to accept cash, credit and debit cards, reloadable prepaid cards, EBT cards, and the digital wallet supported by a smart phone or smart watch. OMNY cards can now be purchased and reloaded from the OMNY card

[p1620_c0001] vending machines, online, or at more than 2000 retail locations across the New York metropolitan area.
### [p1620_c0002] MTA Commuter Railroads (LIRR and MNR)
- [p1620_c0003] a. Transportation Amenities - Transportation amenities are items of comfort and convenience made available to railroad customers. Station amenities provided are generally consistent across the service territory but may vary based on a station's daily ridership, length of platform, and size of station, and may be affected or constrained by physical layout, available space, and utility infrastructure constraints (e.g., local commercial electric power availability). Amenities onboard trains include heating and air conditioning, interior lighting, bathrooms, baggage racks, and public address systems. All trains regardless of car type (coaches or multiple units) and method of propulsion (diesel or electric) are equipped with similar amenities.
### [p1620_c0004] XII. MONITORING OF TRANSIT SERVICE
[p1620_c0005] MTA's Department of Diversity and Civil Rights ("DDCR") oversees, provides guidance, and monitors agency activities to ensure full implementation and compliance with MTA's Title VI Plan. DDCR is the designated liaison to respond to Title VI compliance audits and reviews conducted by the FTA and conducts periodic compliance assessments to determine whether the transit service provided to low-income communities and minority users is consistent with the objectives of the Title VI Circular.
[p1620_c0006] While the Title VI Plan submission is on a three-year cycle and reflects a single consolidated MTA-wide report, DDCR's monitoring of the MTA operating agencies is ongoing, generally including annual reviews and quarterly meetings with the MTA Agencies. Additional oversight activities are initiated as circumstances warrant, such as analyses of anticipated major service and/or fare changes.
[p1620_c0007] The MTA Agencies annually submit the results of random samples comparing the quality and level of services that are provided to minority vs. non-minority and low-income vs. non-low-income communities to DDCR. Each MTA Agency utilizes the following service monitoring procedures for these submittals.
### [p1620_c0008] 1. Level and Quality of Service Methodology
[p1620_c0009] Each MTA Agency implements service guidelines for the communities to which it provides transportation. Commuter rail and subway service are sampled on a station or routes. Racial and income levels for each station are based on the population makeup and Census tracts located within a set radius of a system access point (.25 miles for LIRR and MNR, and 2.5 miles for NYCT Subway). For surface transportation (NYCT Bus and MTA Bus), the sample population includes those Census tracts located within 2.5 miles of the established bus routes for the particular MTA Agency.
[p1620_c0010] Each MTA Agency submits to DDCR, on an annual basis, the following information:
[p1620_c0011] _New York Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA) - Title VI Plan Submission 2026_
[p1620_c0012] _Page 8 of 16_
[p1620_c0013] _Master Page # 1620 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 1621

- [p1621_c0001] • A statistically valid sample of bus routes and rail stations or routes. • An assessment of the performance of each route operating within the sample area, using the applicable service guidelines. • In cases where the performance fails to meet the service guidelines, a statement describing the reason the discrepancy exists and the corrective action to be taken on observed shortfalls.
### [p1621_c0002] 2. Title VI Analysis of Customer Surveys Methodology
[p1621_c0003] The MTA Agencies may assess service equity using the following criteria:
- [p1621_c0004] • Identify an appropriate number of customer survey responses and compare feedback from minority vs. non-minority respondents and from at/below vs. above poverty level respondents; • Determine if there are any significant differences in the responses to travel time, number of transfers, and overall cost of the trip; and • If the MTA Agency concludes that significantly different responses were given by the compared groups, it will address the corrective measures to be taken.
[p1621_c0005] DDCR reviews the above information and any corrective action taken by the MTA Agency and, if necessary, makes recommendations to the applicable MTA Agency.
[p1621_c0006] The following is an assessment of the Title VI Plan for each MTA Agency. The findings in the table indicate whether or not significant differences were found between minority/non-minority and low-income/non-low-income routes in the areas of assessment. If significant differences are found, an explanation is to be provided including action taken to address.

### [p1622_c0002] XIII. COLLECTING AND REPORTING DEMOGRAPHIC DATA AND PROFILE MAPS
[p1622_c0003] During this Title VI Plan reporting period, the MTA collected demographic data, including Demographic and Service Profile Maps and Charts, 5 in order to determine the extent to which members of minority groups are beneficiaries of the MTA programs. Prior to any proposed major service reductions or eliminations, the MTA prepared demographic and service profile maps and charts as shown in Exhibit 12 .
[p1622_c0004] As required under Circular 4702.1B, after the 2010 Decennial Census, the MTA updated its demographic and service profile maps using Geographic Information System (GIS) technology, as follows:
- [p1622_c0005] 1. A base map of the MTA Agencies service areas that overlays Census block groups, including transit routes, fixed guideway alignments, transit stops and stations, depots, maintenance and garage facilities, administrative buildings, and major activity centers, streets and highways. This map portrays the MTA Agencies service area, fixed guideways (the MTA Agencies rail system), transit stations (rail stations and park/ride lots), bus routes, operations and maintenance facilities, and administrative facilities. Major roads, expressways, and ridership generators (high schools/colleges and hospitals) are also shown. 2. A map highlighting facilities that were recently replaced, improved or are scheduled (projects identified in planning documents) for an update in the next five years was also created. This map shows all of the layers from the Minority Populations and Fixed Transit Facilities Map, with color-coded highlights to indicate recent and upcoming facility improvements. The information on this map is used to monitor transit facility improvements and ensure equitable improvements between minority and non-minority areas. 3. A demographic map that plots the information listed in (1) above and also shades those Census block groups where the percentage of the total minority population residing in these areas exceeds the average percentage of minority populations for the service area as a whole. This map shows the basic transit system elements from the base map with an overlay of minority census block groups that exceed the service area average. The data shown on this map is used to monitor equitable provision of transit services, particularly bus routes. 4. Map overlaying minority populations with fixed transit facilities, such as bus shelters, transit stations, and fixed guideways. This map shows all layers from the base map, plus a layer of bus shelter locations, with an overlay of minority census block groups that exceed the service area average. The information on this map is used to monitor the equitable distribution of bus shelters and other passenger amenities.
[p1622_c0006] _5 Connecticut Department of Transportation (CDOT) is mentioned in the Plan's overview, but not included on the Demographic Service Profile Maps and Charts. CDOT conducts the Title VI program requirements for MNR's New Haven Line (excluding NYS Stations) as well as the 3 Branch Lines in Connecticut._

## Page 1623

- [p1623_c0001] 5. A map depicting block groups where the percentage of the total low-income population residing in these areas exceeds the average percentage of low-income populations for the service area as a whole. This map shows the basic transit system elements from the base map with an overlay of low-income census block groups that exceed the service area average. Per Circular 4702.1B, the data on this map was used to identify low-income areas for environmental justice considerations.
### [p1623_c0002] XIV. DEMOGRAPHICS AND TRAVEL PATTERNS
[p1623_c0003] The specific tasks summarized in this section include the collection of customer feedback, system usage and demographics, and fare payment methods. Departments responsible for the collection and analysis of modal ridership included LIRR's Strategic Investments staff, MNR Service Planning staff, NYCT and MTA Bus Operations Planning and the MTA HQ Market Research group. Surveys captured customer information for both the minority and non-minority populations, including but not limited to:
- [p1623_c0004] • Riders' opinion of the quality of service they receive. • Demographics and travel patterns. • Identification of bus routes and rail stations with high percentage of minority and/or general rider usage. • Percentage of riders with language barriers. • Analyses of the impact of changes in fares. • Race and Ethnicity. • Information on Language Proficiency and Language spoken at home. • Household size and household income. • The fare payment type and media usage.
[p1623_c0005] The Spring 2025 MTA Customers Count Survey was conducted in April 2025 and received over 94,000 responses. The survey targeted customers who use any of the MTA's modes of transit: subways, buses, commuter rail, Staten Island Railway, and Access-a-Ride Paratransit. Outreach included offering the survey in nine languages, advertising in print and online media, and promoting the survey on digital signage throughout our transit system. The MTA also offered it to customers via telephone. The 2025 survey results are provided as Exhibit 13 and can also be found at the following link: MTA Customers Count Spring 2025 Survey Results (If the hyperlink does not work, copy and paste the following web address into your browser: https://www.mta.info/article/mta-customers-count-spring-2025-survey-results )
[p1623_c0006] _New York Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA) - Title VI Plan Submission 2026_
[p1623_c0007] _Page 11 of 16_
[p1623_c0008] _Master Page # 1623 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 1624

### [p1624_c0001] XV. MAJOR SERVICE CHANGES JULY 1, 2022 to June 30, 2025
[p1624_c0002] The FTA Title VI Circular requires that transit providers which are in an urbanized area with a population of more than 200,000 and which operate 50 or more vehicles in peak service evaluate the equity impacts of proposed major service and fare changes on minority and low-income populations. To comply with this requirement the MTA utilizes its “major service change” policy to determine when an equity analysis is performed.
[p1624_c0003] From July 1, 2022, through June 30, 2025, the MTA implemented a total of three major service changes. A Service Equity analysis was completed for each project and is provided in Exhibits 14a – 14e . The Service Equity analyses for the major service change projects are summarized below:
- [p1624_c0004] • Queens Bus Network Redesign - MTA Bus Company and NYCT • East Side Access/Grand Central Madison (GCM) 6 – LIRR • Mets-Willet Point Stations (MWP) – LIRR
### [p1624_c0005] Queens Bus Network Redesign
[p1624_c0006] The MTA launched its Bus Network Redesign project to comprehensively review bus service patterns in each borough and better align service with current and future travel demands. In partnership with the New York City Department of Transportation and the riding public, the MTA ensured that affected communities had meaningful opportunities to provide input on the redesigned bus networks.
[p1624_c0007] The Queens Bus Network Redesign represents a historic effort to modernize transit in the borough by creating a more reliable and efficient bus system. Over the past five years, the MTA developed and refined proposals through an iterative process that incorporated extensive public feedback, addressed rider needs, modernized service, and improved operational efficiency for the workforce.
[p1624_c0008] The Proposed Final Plan Addendum was approved by the MTA Board on January 29, 2025. The Addendum reflects the months of community input the MTA received on the Proposed Final Plan and also included an Equity Evaluation. The changes were phased in by route beginning June 29, 2025, and concluding August 31, 2025. The Title VI analysis showed that the Queens Bus Network Redesign Proposal would result in no disparate impact or disproportionate burden on either the minority or the below-poverty populations served by these routes. See Exhibit 14a .
[p1624_c0009] _6 The MTA’s 2023 Title VI submission stated that the service equity analysis for the Major Service Change originating from the East Side Access project would be included in the 2026 Title VI Plan submission. Following the completion of the East Side Access project in 2023, LIRR began full revenue service to Grand Central Madison (GCM), which constituted a Major Service Change requiring a service equity analysis (see Exhibits 14b and 14d)._
[p1624_c0010] _New York Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA) - Title VI Plan Submission 2026_
[p1624_c0011] _Page 12 of 16_
[p1624_c0012] _Master Page # 1624 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 1625

### [p1625_c0001] Grand Central Madison (GCM)
[p1625_c0002] As referenced in Section X of this report, LIRR began full revenue service to Grand Central Madison (GCM) in February 2023 as a result of the completion of the East Side Access project. The new GCM service constituted a Major Service Change in accordance with adopted MTA service guidelines. Draft schedules were published on June 2, 2022. Three virtual public information sessions were held in June and July 2022, and three virtual public hearings were held in July and August 2022 to solicit public comment. Service changes related to the opening of GCM were regularly discussed at Board and Committee meetings leading up to full service commencing in February 2023. On February 8, 2023, the MTA formally announced the start date, and a presentation was made to the MTA Board on February 23, 2023. LIRR found that the proposed service plan associated with the opening of East Side Access had no disparate impact or disproportionate burden on minority and/or low-income riders served by LIRR. See Exhibits 14b and 14d .
### [p1625_c0003] Mets-Willett Point Station (MWP)
[p1625_c0004] On April 24, 2023, full service was made permanent at Mets-Willett Point Station (MWP). Mets-Willett Point is designated a minority station and an above-poverty station and full service to Mets-Willett Point Station constituted a Major Service Change in accordance with adopted MTA Service Guidelines. LIRR conducted a service equity analysis and found that the implementation of full-time service to Mets-Willets Point Station had no disparate impact or disproportionate burden on minority and/or low-income riders served by the LIRR. See Exhibits 14c and 14e .
### [p1625_c0005] XVI. FARE CHANGES JULY 1, 2022 – JUNE 30, 2025
### [p1625_c0006] NYCT Subway, NYCT Bus, and MTA Bus Company
[p1625_c0007] The MTA has a biennial process, which has been in place since 2010, in which it reviews all fares, crossing charges, and related policies. The process begins within the Office of Management and Finance which review the agency's overall budgetary and revenue needs. Based on these findings, a determination is made regarding whether a fare increase is necessary, and if so, the amount required to achieve the budgetary goals.
[p1625_c0008] During the plan period, the following fare changes were implemented:
[p1625_c0009] On December 21, 2022, the Board adopted the 2023 – 2026 Financial Plan, which included a 5.5% increase in fare and toll revenue and authorized agency staff to proceed with the steps necessary to consider, but not implement, proposals for fare and toll increases. As a part of the Fiscal Year 2024 State budget agreement, MTA was able to reduce the planned 5.5% increase in fare revenue to 4%.
[p1625_c0010] The MTA Fare Free Bus Pilot ran from September 24, 2023 – September 1, 2024. Pursuant to FTA guidance, if a promotional or temporary fare lasts longer than six months, an equity analysis must be conducted. However, because the MTA obtained a six (6) month extension from the FTA for this pilot program, no Title VI Fare Equity Assessment was required or performed.
[p1625_c0011] _New York Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA) - Title VI Plan Submission 2026_
[p1625_c0012] _Page 13 of 16_
[p1625_c0013] _Master Page # 1625 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 1626

[p1626_c0001] In May 2023, the MTA announced proposed fare changes on all modes. Notices advising the public of proposed changes in fares and dates for the public hearings on such proposed changes were posted throughout the system. An informational web site was created to provide the public with information on the proposed changes. Fare Equity Analyses were completed for the proposed changes, and the public was invited to comment through virtual and in-person hearings, telephone lines, email, and online. From June 22-26, 2023, the MTA Board held four hybrid public hearings to receive public comments on the proposed fare changes. The MTA board approved the fare changes in May 2023 with the fare increases implemented in August of 2023.
[p1626_c0002] The equity analyses for the approved fare changes are included in Exhibit 15 .
### [p1626_c0003] LIRR
[p1626_c0004] From July 1, 2022, through June 30, 2025, LIRR implemented two fare changes.
### [p1626_c0005] 2023 Fare Changes
[p1626_c0006] On July 19, 2023, the following changes to LIRR fares and fare policy were approved and adopted by the Board:
- [p1626_c0007] • Monthly and Weekly tickets would increase up to 4.5%; however, monthly ticket fares would not exceed $500. • The discount for traveling off-peak would be standardized to 26% across both railroads. (Until that point, MNR's discount was 25% and LIRR's was 27.5%). • All other ticket types would increase up to 10%; however, any ticket increase greater than 6% would be held to a maximum increase of $0.50 per trip. • LIRR would continue to offer UniTicket fares to travel on both commuter rail and connecting bus services. Changes in those fares would be based on changes to weekly and monthly transit fares. • Introduction of a new Far Rockaway Ticket for travel from the LIRR Far Rockaway station, at the same City Ticket prices. • The MTA also considered discontinuing both the $5 One-Way LIRR Atlantic Ticket and $62 LIRR Weekly Atlantic Ticket pilots, as well as the 20-Trip Peak Ticket. For the purposes of the LIRR fare equity analysis, the analysis was performed both with and without the one-way and weekly Atlantic Tickets and 20-Trip Peak Ticket. Under both scenarios, no disparate impact or disproportionate burden was found.
[p1626_c0008] LIRR found that the proposed 2023 fare changes had no disparate impact or disproportionate burden on minority and/or low-income riders served by LIRR. The equity analyses and approved fare changes are included in Exhibit 15 .
[p1626_c0009] _New York Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA) - Title VI Plan Submission 2026_

[p1626_c0011] _Master Page # 1626 of 1629 - Diversity Committee Meeting 3/23/2026_

## Page 1627

### [p1627_c0001] 2024 Fare Changes
[p1627_c0002] In April 2024, the MTA Board approved a temporary fare pilot promotion discount of 10% on monthly tickets for travel within New York City on the commuter railroads. These promotional fares went into effect on June 25, 2024.
[p1627_c0003] LIRR performed an equity analysis pursuant to FTA guidance and found that all outcomes demonstrated differences that were statistically insignificant – thus no mitigating action would be required by the LIRR. Ultimately, the agency determined that this fare pilot program did not have a disparate impact or disproportionate burden on the minority and/or low-income populations served by the LIRR.
[p1627_c0004] The equity analyses for the approved fare hikes as well as the promotional fares that were ultimately discontinued are included in Exhibit 15 .
### [p1627_c0005] MNR
[p1627_c0006] On July 19, 2023, the following changes to MNR fares and fare policy were approved and adopted by the Board:
- [p1627_c0007] • Monthly and Weekly tickets would increase up to 4.5%; however, monthly ticket fares would not exceed $500. • The discount for traveling off-peak would be standardized to 26% across both railroads. • All other ticket types would increase up to 10%; however, any ticket increase greater than 6% would be held to a maximum increase of $0.50 per trip. • Metro-North would continue to offer UniTicket fares to travel on both commuter rail, connecting bus and ferry services. Changes in those fares would be based on changes to weekly and monthly transit fares. • Policies regarding the calculation of onboard fares and refunds would remain unchanged. • For west of Hudson services in New York State operated by New Jersey Transit, Metro-North proposed no increases for any ticket types. • The MTA also considered discontinuing the 20-Trip Peak Ticket. For the purposes of the MNR fare equity analysis described in Section 2, the analysis was performed both with the 20-Trip Peak Ticket and without. Under both scenarios, there was no disparate impact or disproportionate burden.
[p1627_c0008] MNR found that the proposed 2023 fare changes had no disparate impact or disproportionate burden on minority and/or low-income riders served by MNR. The equity analyses for the approved fare hikes as well as the promotional fares that were ultimately discontinued are included in Exhibit 15 .
[p1627_c0009] _New York Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA) - Title VI Plan Submission 2026_

## Page 1628

### [p1628_c0001] XVII. CONCLUSION
[p1628_c0002] The above information and accompanying exhibits reflect the MTA's efforts to document compliance with FTA Title VI guidance and requirements in effect during the applicable plan period, as set forth in FTA Circulars 4702.1B and 4703.1 and Federal Transit Laws. This documentation was submitted to the FTA through the Transit Award Management System (TrAMS) on March 30, 2026.
